title: Dynamically required files (seemingly) no longer run on the same process
body:
### Run `next info` (available from version 12.0.8 and up)

```
    Operating System:
      Platform: darwin
      Arch: arm64
      Version: Darwin Kernel Version 21.2.0: Sun Nov 28 20:28:41 PST 2021; root:xnu-8019.61.5~1/RELEASE_ARM64_T6000
    Binaries:
      Node: 16.13.1
      npm: 8.1.2
      Yarn: 1.22.17
      pnpm: N/A
    Relevant packages:
      next: 12.0.10
      react: 17.0.2
      react-dom: 17.0.2
```

### What version of Next.js are you using?

12.0.10

### What version of Node.js are you using?

16.3.1

### What browser are you using?

Chrome

### What operating system are you using?

macOS

### How are you deploying your application?

next start

### Describe the Bug

When dynamically requiring/importing files, the source code seems to run on different a process. Suppose if you declare a global variable and check its value, the one imported into the "main process" will be different from the one imported into the dynamically required file.

By dynamically requiring files, I am referring to either of these approaches:
```js
require(`./path/to/${fileName}`)
await import(`./path/to/${fileName}`)
```

#### Concept
1. First, declare a `new Date().toISOString()` global variable in a `constants.ts` file.
2. Then import it into say, one of the files in `pages/api/`.
3. Then from `pages/api/`, dynamically require another file.
4. That "another file" should also import the global variable.
5. Then, log the global variable in `pages/api/` and that "another file".
6. Since it's a global variable, you'd expect it to be declared once only, but the values end up being different.

Note: This doesn't happen in v11, as far as I've noticed, it's only happening in v12.

### Expected Behavior

That global variable when logged in multiple places, should have the same value.


### To Reproduce

1. Clone [this](https://github.com/glyph-cat/nextjs-dynamic-require-inconsistency) repository.
2. Run `yarn install`.
3. Run `yarn dev`.
4. Make a GET request with this url (can be from Postman or any browser, but I used Postman):
```
http://localhost:3000/api/greet?templateName=hello&personName=John%20Doe
```
5. Compare the value of `RUNTIME_ID` from files in the `~templates` folder and in `pages/api/greet`.
6. Stop the process, run `yarn add next@11.1.4` and `yarn dev` again, then repeat steps 4 to 5.

Notes:
* React's Context API is used to demonstrate how this can actually be an issue. For example, the values passed into the Context is not received in the dynamically required templates because they seem to "run on different processes".
* This might be an edge case, but I am relying on React as a templating tool to render huge amounts of text, so whether this is really a bug or an intended feature of v12, I hope my reason for opening this issue is legitimate to some extent. `(￣▽￣)`

<hr/>

#### Example output

Expected:
```json
{
    "renderedOutput": "Hello, John Doe. (RUNTIME_ID is 2022-02-14T03:43:34.782Z)",
    "RUNTIME_ID": "2022-02-14T03:43:34.782Z"
}
```

Actual:
```json
{
    "renderedOutput": "Hello, Unknown. (RUNTIME_ID is 2022-02-14T03:43:34.789Z)",
    "RUNTIME_ID": "2022-02-14T03:43:34.782Z"
}
```

title: Regex for assetPrefix in next.config.js
body:
Hello! We are facing a problem with a reverse proxy and how we serve back our assets for the first document load.

Our nginx proxy requires us to have a query param to do a proxy over to the appropriate application in our infrastructure. We can access the document via `https://test.com/helloworld?custom=true`. However, after the document loads it requests all our next static assets however the query param is not there, since it didn't create the urls with it during build time. We do use an assetPrefix in our next.config.js configuration `assetPrefix=/helloworld/`. Requests are shown like this:

`https://test.com/helloworld/*?custom=true/_next/static/chunks/webpack.js?ts=13513561613613616`

I wanted to do something like `assetPrefix=/helloworld/*?custom=true` so that it can be `https://test.com/helloworld/_next/static/chunks/webpack.js?ts=13513561613613616&custom=true`

Is there anyway to do this part of next.config.js? I don't believe we can leverage `rewrites()` since our proxy gets hit first.

title: Edge function rewrite causes Link prefetch to throw errors
body:
Hi,

We're currently facing a lot of issues by following one of the official demos found here: [https://edge-mug.vercel.app/edge](https://edge-mug.vercel.app/edge)

**Our use case:**
`www.example.com/url-example` always gets a rewrite to `www.example.com/url-example/a` or` www.example.com/url-example/b` behind the scenes.

**Our issue:**
It causes the Link component to throw a lot of prefetch errors because it's trying to fetch `www.example.com/url-example`, which doesn't exist.

The logical thing to do would be to use the Link component as follows, so the component would know the real url and can prefetch accordingly:

```
<Link
    href="www.example.com/url-example/a"
    as="www.example.com/url-example"
  >
```

You would expect that the Link component now knows the real URL, but in reality it still tries to prefetch the `as` property.
Does anyone have a temporary fix for this problem? Currently there is no way to turn the prefetching off entirely (it's always viewport or hover).

Kind regards,
Thomas

Is it ok? Because it looks like we don't have any other problems exept maybe slow image loading. Any thoughts? We get all data using graphQl requests to headless wordpress in getStaticProps, all pages were made in the similar way, could it be caused by regeneration of next/image if I didn't set minimumCacheTTL in config? ``` interface CategoryPageProps { head: string menu: MainMenuTypes data: ICategory footerCats: FooterCatReturnType } const CategoryPage: FC = ({ menu, data, footerCats }) => { return ( ) } export default CategoryPage export const getStaticProps = async ({ params }) => { console.log('GET STATIC PROPS CATEGORY') const menuData = await new MenuResources().getMainMenu(MAIN_MENU_ID) const footerCats = await getFooterCategories() const currentCat = await new CategoryResources().getCategorieBySlug( params.category ) const rankMathHead = await new RankMathResources().getHead(currentCat.link) let data if (currentCat.id === CATEGORY.MEDIZINALCANNABIS) data = await getMainCategoryData(currentCat.id) else if (currentCat.id === CATEGORY.NEWS) data = await getNewsCategoryData(currentCat.id) else data = await getSimpleCategoryData(currentCat.id) return { props: { head: rankMathHead, data: { categoryId: currentCat.id, categoryData: data, title: currentCat.name }, menu: menuData, footerCats: footerCats }, revalidate: 300 } } export const getStaticPaths = async () => { const allCategories = await new CategoryResources().getAllCategories(100) const mainCats = allCategories.filter( (el) => !el.parent && el.id !== CATEGORY.UNCATEGORIZED ) return { paths: mainCats.map((cat) => ({ params: { category: cat.slug } })), fallback: false } } ``` Server params: 2 cores processor 2.1GHz, 2Gb RAM

{
  "name": "my-own-ui-library",

TypeError [ERR_INVALID_MODULE_SPECIFIER]: Invalid module "file:///Users/pavel/Dev/github//examples/with-nextjs/node_modules/next/dist/bin/next"

title: Streaming ISR
body:
### Describe the feature you'd like to request

Recently with the release of [Next.js V12.1](https://nextjs.org/blog/next-12-1) came along on-demand ISR updating. It would be very nice if there was a streaming-based approach to ISR, meaning whenever the content is invalidated, the components using the data from the `getStaticProps` automatically re-render with the new data.

### Describe the solution you'd like

Ideally, no code changes should be required. This could be made a config option if need be. 

I see this being very useful in various use cases, for example, a Github Issues Client. My app currently uses ISR and now updates on-demand, but would be really nice if the component that renders the comments would update whenever the data is invalidated so that it is updated in real-time.

### Describe alternatives you've considered

Just refresh the page, however, if you refresh the page while editing text, you will lose that progress. This is a very flawed workaround.

You can see the repo here: https://github.com/solaxds/polyverse-mumbai-testnet

title: Shared ISR storage and revalidation of multiple instances
body:
Hi

I am very excited to see [On-demand Incremental Static Regeneration](https://nextjs.org/blog/next-12-1#on-demand-incremental-static-regeneration-beta) is finally here 🎉.

So now, that this is possible: How can I invalidate the cache of multiple Next.js instances at once? Is there a way I can share the ISR cache between multiple Next.js instances? I use multiple Next.js instances for scaling (SSR/ISR are both used at the moment in a simple docker compose + Traefik setup). 

Am I correct that only an API handler can invalidate the cache? My plan would be to create a simple job that polls a shared storage which holds paths that need to be validated. But this is more challenging if we can only do it in a request context.

title: Bug with on-demand revalidation? Both the old and new versions of the page are showing
body:
I just enabled the new on-demand revalidation feature in NextJS 12.1.0 yesterday.

It looks like calling the API to revalidate pages is working, but sometimes I still see the old version when visiting a page.

As in, I revalidate the page and I navigate to it -- I see the old page. Refreshing the page brings the new version, buf if I navigate away and then back then I get the old one again.

So the only way to produce the new page is to navigate to it and click the refresh button. But navigating to it normally shows the old page.

This also happened when testing in a new incognito window, I believe that rules out any browser caching.

It's as if the edge cache is not being cleared properly after revalidation or something.

Is anyone else seeing this?
author: kristjanmar

title: Support tsconfig paths without baseUrl
body:
### Describe the feature you'd like to request

Typescript supports `paths` [without specifying `baseUrl`](https://github.com/microsoft/TypeScript/pull/40101). This is interesting when you want to use some path aliases without allowing bare imports to resolve from the project root.

### Describe the solution you'd like

next.js already supports `paths` with `baseUrl`. It should just follow what typescript does and resolve `paths` from the config location when `baseUrl` is not specified.

I opened a PR to support this behavior: https://github.com/vercel/next.js/pull/34926

### Describe alternatives you've considered

Configure `baseUrl`

title: Hard crash when applying suggested tsconfig.json in read-only environments
body:
### Verify canary release

- [X] I verified that the issue exists in Next.js canary release

### Provide environment information

```
    Operating System:
      Platform: linux
      Arch: x64
      Version: #1 SMP Wed Jun 16 23:47:55 UTC 2021
    Binaries:
      Node: 16.13.0
      npm: 8.1.0
      Yarn: 1.22.17
      pnpm: N/A
    Relevant packages:
      next: 12.0.11-canary.21
      react: 17.0.2
      react-dom: 17.0.2
```

### What browser are you using? (if relevant)

N/A

### How are you deploying your application? (if relevant)

N/A

### Describe the Bug

In some build environments (CI, Bazel, ...) the source code may be mounted at a read-only path. If next attempts to modify the tsconfig.json file it'll crash out the build with an error such as:

```
[Error: EACCES: permission denied, open '<snip>/test-next/nextjs-blog/tsconfig.json'] {
  errno: -13,
  code: 'EACCES',
  syscall: 'open',
  path: '<snip>/test-next/nextjs-blog/tsconfig.json'
}
```

### Expected Behavior

Not crashing. I'm happy that Next has opinions and helpful onboarding, but the error message has too little info and isn't actionable. Also, I'd expect the process to continue as long as it's not a user request to create the file -- I'd happily have a long error log due to missing an option, and find a warning about tsconfig.json at the top than a random undiagnosable crash. 

### To Reproduce

1. Follow the get started tutorial up until you're able to `$ npm run dev`.
2. `$ touch pages/foobar.ts`
3. `$ npm install --save-dev typescript @types/react @types/node`
4. `$ npm run build` so next.js generates a `tsconfig.json`
5. Edit `tsconfig.json` and remove a compiler option -- I assume all trigger it, but I removed the `moduleResolution` option.
6. `$ chmod -w tsconfig.json`
7. `$ npm run build`

Crash here: https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/canary/packages/next/lib/typescript/writeConfigurationDefaults.ts#L174. I assume it'll also crash here if it's first-time setup in a read-only environment: https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/canary/packages/next/lib/typescript/writeConfigurationDefaults.ts#L98

I have solve this by just create an custom express server and run axios request to home page to trigger this event after server starts

but in my case iam using **pm2** for production and wen i create more than 2 cluster the first cluster who trigger this event wen i call axios request for my domain and the other clusters still waiting for 3-7 sec at first visit

title: Revalidate array of paths – On demand revalidate
body:
### Describe the feature you'd like to request

It would be great if unstable_revalidate could accept an array of paths. 

A lot of time content is referenced on other pages. By only updating the main page via i.e. a webhook, stale content can be shown on other pages. 

I.e. updating the phone number of a contact person. We both want to update the /person/<name> but we also might want to update pages where the person is referenced such as /solutions/finance.

### Describe the solution you'd like

Allow array of paths in unstable_revalidate.

``await res.unstable_revalidate(['/person/john/', '/news/article-written-by-tom'])``

### Describe alternatives you've considered

````
// /api/revalidate.ts

 Promise.all(
    paths.map(async (path) => {
      await res.unstable_revalidate(path)
    })
  )
````

This should work, but is probably not very scaleable.

author: Hanskrogh

title: routing behind reverse proxies (use x-forwarded-host for localized routing)
body:
### Describe the feature you'd like to request

Our stack runs a next site in a docker container. We would like to be able to use domain routing for localization. Unfortunately, the `host` header is the URL of the container it's deployed on. We figured out that the `x-forwarded-host` wasn't set, so we did in our proxy. When I dug deeper in the code of how next and the i18n module does the routing, I found that it only looks at the `host` header.

### Describe the solution you'd like

I found https://github.com/vercel/next.js/pull/20662 which adds a config option to trust proxies. This would fix our problems with the headers. 
Or if anyone has a workaround to also include the `x-forwarded-host` in the `getHostname` functions, that could also help.

### Describe alternatives you've considered

I haven't found any other alternative for our particular deployment.
https://github.com/vercel/next.js/pull/20662 would be perfect and I would be happy to make a new PR that also ads this to the i18n module.
author: gijsbotje

title: On-Demand ISR: Revalidate based on data dependencies
body:
Hey, first of all I want to thank the Next.js team for implementing the on-demand ISR feature. This is something I’ve been looking forward to for some time :tada: 

Now that the feature landed I tried to come up with an elegant solution for deciding which pages need to revalidate when a resource changes. Right now I have an app with a lot of pages (10k+) of various types. Each page consumes data from a database and therefore has dependencies on different resources (sometimes nested).

So what I want to say is: **If one of these resources change, it affects multiple pages** and I’d like to find a way to (elegantly) resolve which pages need to revalidate. So far I have two ideas in mind:

1. In the webhook API function, hard code some algorithm that finds the pages that need to revalidate based on the resource that changed (this would include fetching data from the DB).
2. While getting the page data in `getStaticProps` somehow store the resources that are needed by the current params. Then in the API function, run through the stored pages and revalidate them.

Approach 2 sounds a bit nicer to me, because it doesn’t involve fetching from the DB.

I hope this isn't to obscure or specific :sweat_smile: Do you have any ideas how to go about this?

author: theonlydaleking

title: strict-dynamic CSP support for hybrid Next.js apps
body:
Hi,

I made a package [`@next-safe/middleware`](https://www.npmjs.com/package/@next-safe/middleware) that builds upon [`next-safe`](https://github.com/trezy/next-safe), a package for configuring security headers, including `Content-Security-Policy` (CSP).  With `@next-safe/middleware` you can configure `next-safe` via Next 12 middleware.

However, the highlight of the package is **strict-dynamic CSP support for hybrid Next.js apps** on top, something that isn't feasible without middleware. It contains an easily reusable solution, that decides between a [Nonce-based vs. a Hash-based approach for strict CSP](https://web.dev/strict-csp/#step-1:-decide-if-you-need-a-nonce-or-hash-based-csp) on a per route basis.

It is still in an early PoC state and I've been developing it from a private monorepo so far, but already got in touch with the maintainer of `next-safe` (@trezy) and hope this can be brought into a properly tested and well-documented solution.

If you have a project going with Next 12 and have some time and room for experimentation, I'd be glad if you give it a try and give me some feedback on it!

body:
### Verify canary release

- [x] I verified that the issue exists in Next.js canary release

### Provide environment information

Operating System:
    Platform: linux
    Arch: x64
    Version: #1 SMP Fri Apr 2 22:23:49 UTC 2021
Binaries:
    Node: 17.5.0
    npm: 8.4.1
    Yarn: 3.1.1
Relevant packages:
   next: 12.1.1-canary.1
   react: 17.0.2
   react-dom: 17.0.2

### What browser are you using? (if relevant)

-

### How are you deploying your application? (if relevant)

-

### Describe the Bug

When building with `outputStandalone` and Yarn PnP enabled, the dependencies are not copied correctly at all (because pnp has a slightly different file structure).
Only `.yarn/cache` is created in the `standalone` folder, which contains partially (!) unpacked next and yarn.
And so, without node_modules we get this error
```
Error: Cannot find module 'next/dist/server/next-server'
Require stack: /.next/standalone/server.js
```

### Expected Behavior

To copy everything correctly.
This is the only thing that does not work with pnp.  It would be very cool if you could fix it

### To Reproduce

- Clone [reproduction repo](https://github.com/iivankin/next-pnp-reproduction)
- Run `yarn`
- Run `yarn build`
- Go to the `.next/standalone` and verify the error.

author: edizyurdakul

title: Manual revalidate ISG would have support for I18N and dynamic routes
body:
### Describe the feature you'd like to request

Hi NextJS team.

Firstly thanks for on-demand (manually) revalidation support for statically pre-rendered (getStaticProps) pages. You guys already know that we wanted and waited for this feature in hard way really as community.

After release of latest version I tried it immediately with `res.unstable_revalidate ` api. It works in smooth way. But I realized that it works only for provided url directly. I mean let's say I have a blog and I am supporting `en` and `es` languages.

I have `posts/[id]` page structure. Let's say on this post page I am also fetching and showing my total posts count from an external API.
When I add a new post I want to update all old ones with a web-hook. When I trigger revalidate as like `await res.unstable_revalidate('/en/posts/12')` , it revalidates for only post page of with ID 12 and only English version.

It would be really great if I can revalidate for all versions of that page. The I18N part is not hard. If I am supporting for example 4 languages I can loop them and trigger 4 different `res.unstable_revalidate` requests. It is OK.

But the dynamic urls are not that easy. Because you know if I use ISG I am providing some URLs with getStaticPaths as initially but some URLs being prepared on runtime with ISG. I can't know which pages generated on runtime so I can not trigger revalidate request for that pages.

### Describe the solution you'd like

There can be a custom url pattern in `res.unstable_revalidate` API for dynamic url pages.
For example: `await res.unstable_revalidate('/en/posts/*')`  The asterisk character means purge (revalidate) all routes based on `/en/posts/` .

Also it can take an options argument and it includes a flag for purging all I18N routes for that url.

i.e.: `await res.unstable_revalidate('/posts/*', {revalidateAllI18NVersions: true})` 

Now it will revalidate all I18N versions (en, es) and all paths (/posts/1, /posts/2, /posts/3..... all getStaticPaths + generated paths on runtime with ISG).


### Describe alternatives you've considered

Maybe you guys can expose `incrementalCache` to `req.context` on API handler. So, we can revalidate some pages manually in two steps:

1. Remove static generated pages from .next/server/pages
2. Purge `incrementalCache` for related urls.

I am currently doing this but this requires creating a custom server and it makes more complex to handling things. (You can check from here: https://github.com/MetinArslanturk/microfrontend-nextjs-ssr/blob/i18n-integration/next-host/scripts/utils.js ) Handling from API routes would be easier.

Also handling it with directly the new `unstable_revalidate `API would be much much easier.

If you guys check example repo (in i18n-integration branch), You will see why we need this. We are using dynamic microfrontend pieces on pages. When a microfrontend app has new deployment we want to revalidate all related pages.

Thanks from now,
Metin

title: Error occurred prerendering page, undefined import file's variable at build
body:
Hi everyone, ### I am trying to deploy my Next.js app on vercel and I am getting an error (prerender-error) at build time. I have recently implemented locale functionality and the page language change with locale on click of a global button. To serve different languages, I have simply added a JSON file with key-value pairs for each JSX element and when locale changes, a different JSON key is accessed to get the translated value. Like so: `

{lang[locale].topParagraphOne}

` `lang` is how I imported the JSON file (static import) `locale` is gotten from `useRouter` and passed into `lang` to get the top-level key of the JSON file which is a language key. `topParagraphOne` is the actual text content to pass to the JSX and the lowest-level key of the JSON file. _Both languages in the JSON file have identical keys._ Here is how I set up the locale to work with TypeScript: ``` const router = useRouter() const lang: translation = language let locale = 'en' if (router.locale !== undefined) { locale = router.locale } return ... ``` P.S. - `translation` is an imported interface At build I am getting this: ``` Error occurred prerendering page "/about". Read more: https://nextjs.org/docs/messages/prerender-error TypeError: Cannot read property 'topParagraphOne' of undefined ... ``` I have read the prerender-error page on the next.js website and it was not very useful in my cas. Would anyone be able to point me in the direction of how I can solve this issue? Thank you so much, in advance :)

title: Modify path of _preflightRequest
body:
Hi everyone,

I have an issue with all preflight request on production as we use NextJS project in a different root folder and I would like to know if there is a way to modify it before to send the request.

Here more context:
Our domain is used for different purpose: 
- `xxx.com/Y` : Y WebApplication
- `xxx.com/Z` : Z service
- ...

For our public website, we used mainly `xxx.com/:locale/*`: Website developed with NextJS.
To solve the issue with the default locale prefix, we used the method discussed in the discussion https://github.com/vercel/next.js/discussions/18419#discussioncomment-1561577

So at this point, everything is fine, I can imitate in my local machine the same behaviour as production, except when I published on production as all preflight request is performed without the `:locale` so I have:
- `xxx.com/routeA`
- `xxx.com/routeB`
- ...
Instead of
- `xxx.com/:locale/routeA`
- `xxx.com/:locale/routeB`
- ...
Obviously, they lead to 404 errors as only `xxx.com/:locale` traffic are redirected to NextJS project, and others requests to some others services.

From what I can see, the preflight URL is resolved in [packages/next/shared/lib/router/router.ts#L1028](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/canary/packages/next/shared/lib/router/router.ts#L1028) file.
But I don't see any options to enforce some prefix or redefined the "real" root of the project to append directly to the generated URL.

The only current options I can figure out are:
- create a `[locale]` folder and move everything there. That's mean I should disable all locale detection feature and rewrite all `<Link />` url by disabling locale attribute and enforcing the locale management in the href directly. It will require lots of effort to rewrite all of them
- finding a way to disable preflight request to avoid these massive 404 and rely on direct access from user to these page, which will reduce a part of optimisation process provided by NextJS. But I cannot find the option in the documentation except that it's a feature only available on production. I need to investigate more the code (like the [middleware part](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/canary/packages/next/shared/lib/router/router.ts#L1870) ) to see if there is a way for that.

Can you confirm what I can do?

Thank you.

title: Does Next.js perform automatic page refresh?
body:
Does Next.js have a mechanism by which a full page reload is performed under certain conditions or at a given point in time?

The reason for this question stems from a use case that is quite common i.e. when the the app is never refreshed manually by the user and hence is never updated to the latest changes.

In my particular case, the app is a non-SPA audio player and we noticed old versions of the client application of our non-SPA website still logging to our telemetry system, meaning the new releases don't reach 100% of all users.

I'm aware of the possibility in Next.js of programmatically force the router to reload by using `router.reload(window.location.pathname)` or `Router.reload(window.location.pathname)` (depending whether you are on the client or on the server), but I wanted to understand if there was an automatic (and configurable) mechanism that was doing this already.

Thanks for your help.

author: tagoad

title: Removing SSR incompatible package from server bundle
body:
Hi everyone,
We have 3rd party package, we can't control, which uses `window` within top level. Which means on SSR it crashes in the moment when it is imported/required. We don't need the package to do anything on server because it is used only on client (inside `useEffect`). 

Is there a way how to easily omit it from server bundle or disable its import in SSR?

example usage
```tsx
import { fn } from "bad-package"

export Component = () => {
  useEffect(() => {
     fn(...)
  })

  return <>some content</>
}
```


Options we've tried & considered:

### Using `next/dynamic` with `ssr: false`
unfortunately the package isn't component so this doesn't fit well. And when we wrap the whole component it disables its pre-rendering.

### Dynamic import `await import(...)`
this works, but we are building internal tools for our teams and this is very inconvenient because the syntax of using that package gets very ugly because of that.
Other issue with this package is that it isn't bundled as part of the main client bundle and so it creates latency when we call that which is issue on slower networks

### `<NoSSR />`
this can't work because it prevents render but not import, and our package is crashing during import

### Webpack 5 [resolve.alias](https://webpack.js.org/configuration/resolve/#resolvealias) with value `false` (`null-loader` in webpack 4)
This would be ideal and should work from what I understand, but it doesn't.

I'm trying to set 
```ts
config.resolve.alias["bad-package"] = false
```
and while it works for client bundle, it doesn't for server side.

When I check stats from webpack bundle analyzer I can see the module marked as `external` (and so not bundled)(with alias or not) but the code is still importing it and crashing ssr.

Also I've tried to use deprecated `null-loader` with same result.

Any ideas how to make the webpack alias work or some other approach that can help resolve this nicely

author: FezVrasta

title: Can `create-next-app`'s default typescript extend one of the default bases from TypeScript?
body:
### Describe the feature you'd like to request

When you run `create-next-app`, you get the following for the `tsconfig.json`:
```json
{
  "compilerOptions": {
    "target": "es5",
    "lib": ["dom", "dom.iterable", "esnext"],
    "allowJs": true,
    "skipLibCheck": true,
    "strict": true,
    "forceConsistentCasingInFileNames": true,
    "noEmit": true,
    "esModuleInterop": true,
    "module": "esnext",
    "moduleResolution": "node",
    "resolveJsonModule": true,
    "isolatedModules": true,
    "jsx": "preserve",
    "incremental": true
  },
  "include": ["next-env.d.ts", "**/*.ts", "**/*.tsx"],
  "exclude": ["node_modules"]
}
```
While this is fine, it does put a decent amount on the end user to understand if they were to enter this file.

### Describe the solution you'd like

TypeScript's docs specify [default bases for typescript configs](https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/handbook/tsconfig-json.html#tsconfig-bases).

Main benefits:
- standardizes between those using `create-next-app` and those using a bespoke, custom configuration
- centralizes the standards and best practices for the default next configuration

```json
{
  "extends": "@tsconfig/next/tsconfig.json",
  "compilerOptions": {
    // if they override, they override here.
  },
  "include": ["next-env.d.ts", "**/*.ts", "**/*.tsx"],
  "exclude": ["node_modules"]
}
```

### Describe alternatives you've considered

The current solution is working, this is just an enhancement.

body: It'd be great if I could generate my image cache ahead of serving, during the build step. author: tomer183

title: heads ups: don't nest <Head>
body:
Just a heads up, we experienced a weird problem when we updated an older app from next 10 to 12 (12.1).

The symptons where that all buttons did no longer work and we got a lot of warnings, like these:

```
index.js?2f81:14 It looks like there are several instances of `@material-ui/styles` initialized in this application.
This may cause theme propagation issues, broken class names, specificity issues, and makes your application bigger without a good reason.

See https://material-ui.com/r/styles-instance-warning for more info.
```

and same for styled-components:

```
It looks like there are several instances of 'styled-components' 
```


it turned out we had accientially nested a `<Head />` inside a `<Head />` in a customized _document 🤦‍♂️. Removing the inner `<Head />` fixed the problem

I just wanted to tell you this in case anyone else stumbles over this problem.

      react: 17.0.2
      react-dom: 17.0.2

### What browser are you using? (if relevant)

_No response_

### How are you deploying your application? (if relevant)

_No response_

### Describe the Bug

Some libraries ship `es6` code and require to use `next-transpile-modules`.

It could be easy to transpile these by specifiying in `transformIgnorePatterns`, but the default config is not allowing overrides.

https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/cc5345bba852077c332ddc0b75279a0f96d4321a/packages/next/build/jest/jest.ts#L118

### Expected Behavior

Fully replace `transformIgnorePatterns` if it's specified in `resolvedJestConfig`.

### To Reproduce

N/A

title: yarn build cache warning during CI
body:
using GitHub workflow to deploy an SSG nextjs website. this is the caching part in my .`yaml` file:
```yaml
- name: Cache node modules
        uses: actions/cache@v2
        with:
          path: '**/node_modules'
          key: ${{ runner.os }}-modules-${{ hashFiles('**/yarn.lock') }}
```

during `yarn build` i get this warning:

```
warn  - No build cache found. Please configure build caching for faster rebuilds. Read more: https://nextjs.org/docs/messages/no-cache
```

I checked this https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/main/errors/no-cache.md#github-actions that uses this:
```yaml
uses: actions/cache@v2
with:
  path: ${{ github.workspace }}/.next/cache
  key: ${{ runner.os }}-nextjs-${{ hashFiles('**/package-lock.json') }}
```

and this https://github.com/belgattitude/nextjs-monorepo-example/pull/290/commits that uses this:

```yaml
- name: Restore nextjs build web-app from cache
        uses: actions/cache@v2
        with:
          path: ${{ github.workspace }}/apps/web-app/.next/cache
          key: ${{ runner.os }}-nextjs-web-app-${{ hashFiles('**/yarn.lock') }}-${{ hashFiles('apps/web-app/src/**.[jt]sx?') }}
          restore-keys: |
               ${{ runner.os }}-nextjs-web-app-${{ hashFiles('**/yarn.lock') }}-
```

my question: what is the right way to fix the `yarn build` warning about the `no cache build found`

Playwright can spin up an instance of next dev server automatically before running tests by passing the `webServer` config object. Additionally, Playwright allows to capture full screen screenshots for visual diffing. I noticed such screenshots to be a bit flaky, because at times Next's dev indicator gets in the way, dirtying up the final screenshot.

I know I can disable the indicator by passing a `devIndicators` object to next.config.js and I know I can selectively pass different configuration objects based on phases. What I couldn't figure out is a way to keep devIndicators during development and disabling it when next is run by Playwrights. Any hints?

title: Using getStaticPaths with [...slug]
body:
Happy Friday all! I have a dynamic catch all path named [...table].js in the root of the pages director. I am using getStaticProps and therefore I need to use getStaticPaths. I cannot figure out how to configure the return paths in getStaticPaths.  I see nothing in the docs that mention how to do this. Specifically the nextjs error that my table needs to be an array. Which I assumed meant it should match the format of how the query params are sent to a [...slug].js route.

my url  will be /path1/path2/path3. So 3 dynamic variables.

```
export async function getStaticPaths() {
  console.log("getstaticpaths ran")
  return {
    paths: [
      { params: { table: [" path1/path2/path3"] } },
    ],
    fallback: true,
  }
}
```

The above example gets nextjs to stop sending errors. And with fallback true getStaticProps works with /path1/path2/path3. However If I change fallback:false, I get a 404 because my getStaticPath is not correct and the html is not pre-rendered.

I have tried the example below as well. I thought this would be the correct syntax since it matches the query params sent in route.

```
    paths: [
      { params: { table: [" path1", "path2","path3"] } },
    ],

```

So can someone show me the correct return object for getStaticPath?  

Please and thank you!
author: 2stash

title: Unable to opt out of builtin polyfills
body:
### Verify canary release

- [X] I verified that the issue exists in Next.js canary release

### Provide environment information

Next: 10.2.3
Node: 14.15.4

### What browser are you using? (if relevant)

Chrome 65

### How are you deploying your application? (if relevant)

_No response_

### Describe the Bug

Application needs to support older versions of Chrome, in this example v60-68. The app uses a video player package built on jQuery 3.4. The video player uses the method `$.replaceWith()` or `$.replaceAll()` which is where the error occurs with the Next.js `array.flat` polyfill.

```
  var flat = arr.flat ? function(array) {
    return arr.flat.call(array); // Next's polyfill errors here and needs to be disabled so jQuery can fall through
  } : function(array) {
    return arr.concat.apply([], array); 
  }
```

If the [Next.js array.flat polyfill](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/0af3b526408bae26d6b3f8cab75c4229998bf7cb/packages/next-polyfill-module/src/index.js#L53) is removed and the `polyfill-module.js` file is rebuilt the error is no longer present.

Additionally if the Next polyfill is replaced with [`array.flat` polyfill from Array Flat Polyfill](https://github.com/jonathantneal/array-flat-polyfill/blob/master/src/polyfill-flat.js) the error also does not occur.

The bug in this instance is the default solution introduces conflicts with other popular libraries with no obvious way to gracefully opt out.


### Expected Behavior

Ideally would like to have Next default array.flat polyfill  work in all situations. Or at the very least a way to disable the provided polyfills through a `next.config` flag such as `useDefaultPolyfills: boolean`.

### To Reproduce

In Chrome v65

1. Set debugger before using `.replaceWith()`
![Screen Shot 2022-02-25 at 1 02 35 PM](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/3144419/155803022-e3326587-05f6-4c2d-be50-7ac81132eae5.png)

2. Stepping through till jQuery calling `flat(args)`
![Screen Shot 2022-02-25 at 1 03 21 PM](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/3144419/155803086-f625b541-1abb-4373-ba80-8acdaa9a25e9.png)

3. Calls `arr.flat.call(array)` because of the Next polyfill. (Will work if polyfill is removed from `polyfill-module.js`)
![Screen Shot 2022-02-25 at 1 03 30 PM](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/3144419/155803127-eb8228ef-f7c4-49b9-b070-b3cf8042b69a.png)

4. Fails with provided Next.js polyfill for `array.flat`
![Screen Shot 2022-02-25 at 1 05 49 PM](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/3144419/155803295-35d49033-2d3a-4f83-ba7a-a9d13d1a5dc7.png)
author: wsfuller

title: Support multiple callbacks for `router.beforePopState()`
body:
### Describe the feature you'd like to request

Currently, `router.beforePopState()` only supports [a single callback function](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/v12.1.1-canary.3/packages/next/shared/lib/router/router.ts#L1622). Subsequent calls to that function will wipe out any previously supplied callback functions. Perhaps this is intentional, but I'd prefer it to support a variable number of callbacks. I think this would be both functionally and intuitively superior.

My use case is simply that multiple components currently active in the hierarchy may wish to supply their own callback.

### Describe the solution you'd like

1. `beforePopState()` should simply push the supplied callback on to an array
2. It would return a cleanup function so that callees can remove the callback when unmounting (e.g. as a return value from a `useEffect` hook
3. If _any_ of the supplied callbacks return `false`, then the state change would be prevented, thus continuing to support the current ability to prevent a state change.

### Describe alternatives you've considered

I've currently resorted to providing a proxied version of the router via context in my app so that I can augment the functionality in this way.

title: Require help with migrating to NextJS from PHP Symfony
body:
Currently, I'm planning out the migration of an app built with PHP Symfony to NextJS. The goal is to migrate away from Symfony component by component instead of page by page. I'm trying to achieve this through NextJS export, which outputs one HTML file per component. I just have to create a file in pages/components which exports exactly one component. Then I run next build && next export. Each HTML file will be included through NGINX's server side includes functionality. This works well enough that I can migrate something.

However, I'm running into an issue with localstorage syncing between components. Multiple components on one page must be able to subscribe to changes to local storage. I.e. component A should update if a button in Component B is changing the localstorage. How can I let multiple exported components use the same localStorage?

title: POST request in `_middleware.ts`
body: In my middleware function, I need to make a POST request to a GraphQL server. Based on the response, the user may or may not have access to view the page they're requesting. I don't want to use `getServerSideProps` because this auth logic applies to entire route subtrees. For example, if we query the GraphQL API and the user has access to `/a` then they have access to every page under `/a` as well. That would get pretty unwieldy to have to implement the same check in `getServerSideProps` for all of those pages. Middleware is a much better option with it's behavior. I'm aware of the latency repercussions of making this request, but unfortunately it's necessary for my usecase. Specifically, I'm running into an error with the request body.

title: Why `__NEXT_DATA__` is pushed to `window`?
body:
I recently reviewed all global variables of my website. One of them is `__NEXT_DATA__`. I found [this discussion](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/discussions/15117) which explains nicely why the `<script id="__NEXT_DATA__">` is needed, but I just can't understand why is it also pushed to `window` object?

I also identified `__SSG_MANIFEST_CB`, `__SSG_MANIFEST`, `__NEXT_P`, `__NEXT_PRELOADREADY`, `__BUILD_MANIFEST_CB`, `__MIDDLEWARE_MANIFEST_CB`, `__BUILD_MANIFEST`, `__MIDDLEWARE_MANIFEST` and `next` which even exposes Next.js version - not a good security practice. Do they all need to be available to all other scripts and browser extensions?

title: How do I pass a value from a middleware to controllers?
body:
Hi,

Coming from the node.js-express world and working with ts-next.js for the first time, I've been scratching my head as to how I could pass a value from a middleware to the subsequent controllers.

My exact requirement is to carry out token-based authentication in the middleware and pass the retrieved user data to all the subsequent controllers.

In node.js-express, I would assign the details to req.user or add an attribute to res.locals. Neither works here for obvious reasons. What's the way to go here?, other than calling a function in every single controller.

title: Build on Next 12 takes over 30 minutes
body:
### Verify canary release - [X] I verified that the issue exists in Next.js canary release ### Provide environment information ``` Operating System: Platform: darwin Arch: x64 Version: Darwin Kernel Version 21.3.0: Wed Jan 5 21:37:58 PST 2022; root:xnu-8019.80.24~20/RELEASE_X86_64 Binaries: Node: 14.17.4 npm: 7.23.0 Yarn: 1.22.17 pnpm: N/A Relevant packages: next: 12.1.0 react: 17.0.2 react-dom: 17.0.2 ``` ### What browser are you using? (if relevant) _No response_ ### How are you deploying your application? (if relevant) next build ### Describe the Bug **TL;DR**: NextJS 12 build time increases drastically when CSS modules are added --- ## The story Long-time Next user here. Our main website runs entirely on NextJS and the experience has been pretty good so far. Lately we decided it was time to get our hands dirty and update from Next 10 to the new 12 with the SWC compiler and all that implies. Additionally, we decided to start using CSS modules instead of a single gigantic CSS file for the whole app made of a lot of imported, much smaller component styles. The goal is double-fold: improve developer experience and avoid serving unnecessary CSS code on each page to optimise network use. The overall migration process was rather easy. Next 12's development server uses a lot of RAM (min 2GB), but performance is really incredible, especially when hot reloading. However, our build time went from approximately 5 minutes to over 30 minutes. It has become so long in fact that it does not build properly on Heroku anymore. Here's one build log:

├ └ css/ea501070afc3c47b.css 17.3 kB ├ λ /tos 6.64 kB 703 kB ├ λ /travel 14.8 kB 878 kB ├ └ css/d7215ca0310291c6.css 8.97 kB ├ λ /travel/category/[category_id] 3.55 kB 875 kB ├ λ /travel/hotel/[hotel_id] 8.56 kB 907 kB ├ λ /travel/package/[id] 16.3 kB 823 kB ├ └ css/6f082bb6e2fdd2de.css 9.28 kB ├ λ /travel/search 4.73 kB 837 kB └ λ /unsubscribe 3.22 kB 694 kB + First Load JS shared by all 690 kB ├ chunks/framework-2e8d380ab91e9dce.js 46.5 kB ├ chunks/main-339de6c6280cb5e5.js 32.1 kB ├ chunks/pages/_app-596a641a25e82b41.js 608 kB ├ chunks/webpack-e8b2c8bcc23836a5.js 3.49 kB └ css/379a08765d2fe3bf.css 1.05 MB λ (Server) server-side renders at runtime (uses getInitialProps or getServerSideProps) Done in 2022.23s. -----> Caching build Saving 2 cacheDirectories (package.json): - node_modules - .next/cache -----> Pruning devDependencies yarn install v1.22.17 [1/4] Resolving packages... [2/4] Fetching packages... -----> Timed out running buildpack Node.js ! Push failed ```
As you can read near the last line of this log, it took **2,022.23** seconds to build the app oh Heroku —that's just a little bit shy of 34 minutes. On my local machine, with SSD and no CPU-intensive concurrent processes, it takes 1,241.31 seconds —approximately 21 minutes— to build the app after removing the `.next/` folder. I know that Heroku can cache some folders, and you can see that we configured the caching already to include `.next/cache`. However, no cache is available for most of our development branches builds because they are often short-lived, and need to be able to build and run a new branch without leveraging cache. ## The ideas and thoughts It seems the drastically increased build time stems from the CSS modules usage. You can guess from the build log that not all pages are currently leveraging CSS modules since not all of them have corresponding .css files. During development, it seemed that moving CSS code from the global stylesheet to a module increased build time as I went on. Now we're halfway there through the whole CSS refactoring process, and I'm unsure going through with it is a good idea because of this scalability issue. ## The questions Now I guess I have a few questions: 1. how can I profile the build process to understand what is taking the most time to build? 2. are there ways I do not know of that would help getting a faster build process? 3. is there a "soft-limit" regarding CSS modules count? ### Expected Behavior Build time should be similar if not improved over Next 10 or 11 ### To Reproduce - Use CSS modules (384 `*.modules.scss` files in our project currently) - `next build` If profiling the build process myself is too complicated, I guess I could share the project for investigation under an NDA. Unfortunately, it would take too much time for me to try and build using Next 12 without any CSS modules to be able to rule-out CSS modules. However, I strongly believe this is the cause of the increased build time here.

```javascript
/**

```

-  `package.json`

```javascript

{
  "name": "helloworld",

  "private": true,
  "scripts": {
    "dev": "next dev",
    "build": "next build",
    "start": "next start",
    "lint": "next lint",

  },

    "eslint": "8.9.0",

  }
}

```

- Using `npx serve out`, testing these approaches:
  - Doing request directly to `http://localhost:3000/admin` *(working)*
  - Doing request directly to `http://localhost:3000/admin/` *(working)*

- Using `pm2 serve out --no-daemon`, testing these approaches:

  - Doing request directly to `http://localhost:3000/admin/` *(not working - 404 error)*
  - Or clicking from home and use a `next/link` to admin, it is working. *(working)*
  - With using `pm2 serve out --no-daemon --spa` is redirecting all requests to index.html *(expected)* 

Is there any consideration or workaround for pm2 on this scenario or do we need to discard the pm2 option to serve static pages for nextjs framework?

title: Add support for query param forwarding in getStaticProps redirect
body:
### Describe the feature you'd like to request

In our app, we have pages that use `getStaticProps`, ISR and `fallback: blocking`. The route param accepts a `slug` in the format of `[id]-[friendlyName]`.

If the user visits a URL with a correct `id`, but an incorrect `friendlyName`, we want to redirect them to the canonical URL. For example, a request comes in to `/event/123-my-first-event`. We find the event with ID `123`, but notice that the `friendlyName` in the database is actually `my-second-event`. Therefore the canonical URL is `/event/123-my-second-event`. In `getStaticProps` we perform a permanent redirect.

However, during these redirect all query parameters are lost. This is critical as analytics tracking params are frequently appended, and if the user gets redirected we lose all the tracking.

We would like the ability to optionally opt-in to forward the original request's query parameters onto the supplied `destination`.

### Describe the solution you'd like

A new option in redirect to be added, for example;

```js
return {
  redirect: {
    destination: '/event/123-my-second-event',
    permanent: true,
    forwardQueryParams: true
  }
}
```

When this is supplied, the Next router takes the `destination` and appends the query parameters from the original request. For example, a request to `/event/123-my-first-event?foo=bar` becomes `/event/123-my-second-event?foo=bar`

An implementation of this has been developed in a fork and is ready to be submitted as a PR if this idea is approved.

### Describe alternatives you've considered

The only possible alternatives currently would be either;

1. Use `getServerSideProps`, but we lose the caching benefits of `getStaticProps`
2. Create a middleware in front of the page to check for the canonical URL in advance, however this would run on every request and essentially negate the caching benefits

title: So only public environment variables couldn't be destructured, right?
body:
According to the docs:
> Note: To keep server-only secrets safe, Next.js replaces `process.env.*` with the correct values at build time. This means that process.env is not a standard JavaScript object, so you’re not able to use [object destructuring](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/JavaScript/Reference/Operators/Destructuring_assignment). Environment variables must be referenced as e.g. `process.env.PUBLISHABLE_KEY`, not `const { PUBLISHABLE_KEY } = process.env`.

So next.js replaces `process.env.PUBLISHABLE_KEY` with the actual value at build time. I've taken a look at the [source code](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/721dc7bccd0bcdd6f8d6600b0dc30192822a0f3d/packages/next/build/webpack-config.ts#L1288) and as I understood, next.js does this only for environment variables that start with `NEXT_PUBLIC_` + some special ones. So technically we should be able to destructure the environment variables that don't start with `NEXT_PUBLIC_`, am I right? shouldn't the docs clarify it?

title: I was also building a starter kit similar to Vercel's Platform Starter-kit (Nextacular)
body:
Sharing [Nextacular](https://nextacular.co) is an open-source starter kit that will help SaaS founders build full-stack multi-tenant SaaS platforms efficiently and help you focus on developing your core SaaS features. Built on top of popular and modern technologies such as Next JS, Tailwind, Prisma, and Stripe.

It has been in development since January this year and I've been gathering feedback on what are the basic SaaS features that founders should not sweat about to help them focus on building the core of their solutions. The feedback gathered resulted in Nextacular, which is a SaaS starter-kit built on top of modern technologies.

Also, in our startup, our team didn't have a standard way of building our applications, so I thought of spending some time crafting this Workspace-based multi-tenant SaaS starter kit to be deployed on Vercel. This is an open-source initiative to contribute to the development community.

(I do know that there exists Vercel's Platform Starter kit, and I was happy I was invited into the repository for `edge-functions` and `platforms`. I was already building Nextacular before they released the project and had to piece some of these things together with my knowledge about Next.JS and Vercel's APIs. Luckily, the repo invite made me improve the codebase of Nextacular. Besides, Nextacular was built on top of Next.JS, which will be deployed over at Vercel.)

I guess some of the distinguishing factors of Nextacular with Platforms are:
1. We will focus on developer experience and improve on the existing documentation
2. Create more integrations and a community with third-party services
3. Templates + Integrations marketplace, which will be very useful for 
4. Workspace and teams features (out-of-the-box)
5. Payments feature (out-of-the-box)

So many plans up ahead, and I do hope the community will be able to move this project forward as well as an alternative to Platforms. 😅
Thank you!

title: Cannot resolve "fs/promises" error failing build
body:
### Verify canary release

- [X] I verified that the issue exists in Next.js canary release

### Provide environment information

    Operating System:
      Platform: linux
      Arch: x64
      Version: #33-Ubuntu SMP Fri Feb 4 17:03:31 UTC 2022
    Binaries:
      Node: 14.19.0
      npm: 8.5.2
      Yarn: 1.22.17
      pnpm: 6.32.2
    Relevant packages:
      next: 12.1.0 (also tested 12.1.1-canary.6)
      react: 17.0.2
      react-dom: 17.0.2


### What browser are you using? (if relevant)

not relevant

### How are you deploying your application? (if relevant)

Vercel

### Describe the Bug

Since upgrading to Next.js 12.1.0 running `yarn build` produces this error for any import from `"fs/promises"` including pages, their imports and npm packages:

```
yarn run v1.22.17
$ node --version && next build
v14.19.0
info  - Checking validity of types  
info  - Creating an optimized production build  
Failed to compile.

./src/utils/server/getFileList.js
Module not found: Can't resolve 'fs/promises' in '/home/mitsu/git/Projects/fgo-tools/src/utils/server'
```

This bug was particularly hard to pinpoint as these errors look exactly like the ones you would see when accidentally using Node.js 12 instead of 14 and only appear for `next build` and NOT `next dev`. 

I at first suspected Vercel to be using Node.js 12 and after asking on Twitter added `"engines": {"node":"^14"}` to my package.json (which just produced a warning about ^14 not matching 14.x, but oh well) and changed the build command to `node --version && next build` as seen above. This confirmed that the Vercel deployment was indeed using the exact same version as I was using locally, ruling out Vercel as the issue.

### Expected Behavior

The build succeeds as it did with Next.js 12.0.x and earlier versions.

### To Reproduce

- create a new Next.js project using Next.js version 12.1.0 or canary
- add `import { readFile } from "fs/promises";` to `./pages/index.js`
- run the `next build` command

You should get the following output:

```
$ next build
info  - Checking validity of types  
info  - Creating an optimized production build  
Failed to compile.

./pages/index.js
Module not found: Can't resolve 'fs/promises' in '/home/mitsu/git/Temp/reproduce-fs-import-crash/pages'


> Build failed because of webpack errors
```

--------

Edit: Wanted to clarify more clearly that this issue only appears on 12.1.0 and newer, not older versions. The main branch of my project is still on 12.0.x and deploys the same code with no issues.
author: Mitsunee

title: Add idx to router.object
body:
### Describe the feature you'd like to request

I'd like to propose adding [idx](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/a51803624638a626694b03c23b48e373eb586517/packages/next/shared/lib/router/router.ts#L1410) to the [router.object](https://nextjs.org/docs/api-reference/next/router#router-object).`idx` is included in the history state on every new history entry.

This is valuable for reporting purposes such as the number of page views in the current session. 

It's also valuable when building native-like applications using nextjs, particularly for back button behaviors. When replicating a native like experience, it's helpful to be able to check if a programatic `router.back()` would kick the user out of the app, and if so, fallback to another page (like a Homepage). This pattern frequently comes up for back buttons rendered on an error page and settings pages.

### Describe the solution you'd like

A new `idx` field (or perhaps `pageCount` / `pageIndex`?) added to the router object.

### Describe alternatives you've considered

1. Accessing `history.state.idx` directly. This works, but is undocumented and I'm concerned about the field changing or being removed in a next update and application code silently breaking.

2. Another option would be to include a `fallback` option with [router.back](https://nextjs.org/docs/api-reference/next/router#routerback) so that users aren't backed out of the application.

body:
My app's WebSockets connection fails in development mode on port 3000. If I use the app on port 64729, it works. 
To figure out why, I run `vercel dev --debug` in terminal and get this when I access the app on port 3000:

```
> [debug] [2022-03-03T02:19:24.690Z] GET /_next/webpack-hmr

title: with-iron-session SG Shows sensitive information before redirect
body:
### Verify canary release

- [X] I verified that the issue exists in Next.js canary release

### Provide environment information

run `yarn create next-app --example with-iron-session with-iron-session-app`

(you can update next to canary and it does the same thing).

### What browser are you using? (if relevant)

Google Chrome 98.0.4758.102

### How are you deploying your application? (if relevant)

yarn dev

### Describe the Bug

Shows the profile-sg page before redirecting to login page.

It's a split second, but that would be easy to copy data using a parser if it isn't completely dependent on the user being present. I also don't believe the answer is "Wrap the entire component's return in a `user.isLoggedIn` condition" - that's the entire point of using a hook like the example in https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/canary/examples/with-iron-session/lib/useUser.ts. 

### Expected Behavior

redirects to login page without showing the page.

**Reason this is important:**

Example page: [slug].js

You will have a `getStaticProps` and `getStaticPaths` for the page, since (presumably) you will have data that you are fetching based on the slug. This means you cannot use `getServerSideProps` on said page - you must use the SG version. If your API cannot depend on session authentication (in my example, I am using Sanity CMS), it will actually pull and display the data before redirect.

The example only has publicly available data, but if you have sensitive information, this could lead to issues...

Is there any way to prevent this besides wrapping each component that needs authorization in [useUser](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/canary/examples/with-iron-session/lib/useUser.ts)? If so, can you please change the example so that you can prevent the page from loading _without_ a conditional close for `user.isLoggedIn` in each page/component that requires authorization? I'd really like to use this, but I'm going to have to find a different project until the example can be updated... I'm personally having other issues getting `useUser` to work in my project (it keeps redirecting whether the user is logged in or not for some reason), but this is a major deal breaker for me in general.

### To Reproduce

1. run `yarn create next-app --example with-iron-session with-iron-session-app`
2. `cd with-iron-session-app`
3. `yarn dev`
4. navigate to http://localhost:3000/profile-sg before logging in

title: Params should be configurable in next/image cloudinaryLoader
body:
### Describe the feature you'd like to request

**The user should be able to dictate cloudinary params as per their requirements. If not provided, we can fallback to the default params.**

Currently, the user is only able to add `width` and `quality` as params to the `cloudinaryLoader`. For my use case, the cloudinary link should consist of other params for image size, for eg `t_web_ab_2x`. 

For example, People might not use `width` as attribute to specify image dimensions, they could other custom ways like `t_web_ab_4x` etc.

**Source Code reference:** https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/ed43c03ebb164d88657c933be569b499f8e1e074/packages/next/client/image.tsx#L813

```
// Current Implementation
function cloudinaryLoader({
  config,
  src,
  width,
  quality,
}: ImageLoaderProps): string {
  // Demo: https://res.cloudinary.com/demo/image/upload/w_300,c_limit,q_auto/turtles.jpg
  const params = ['f_auto', 'c_limit', 'w_' + width, 'q_' + (quality || 'auto')]
  const paramsString = params.join(',') + '/'
  return `${config.path}${paramsString}${normalizeSrc(src)}`
}
```

### Describe the solution you'd like

The params should be passed as argument to the cloudinaryLoader. We can have some default params if the user hasn't provided any.

Ideal implementation as per me:

```
function cloudinaryLoader({
  config,
  src,
  width,
  quality,
  params
}: ImageLoaderProps): string {
  // Demo: https://res.cloudinary.com/demo/image/upload/w_300,c_limit,q_auto/turtles.jpg
  const cloudinaryParams = params || ['f_auto', 'c_limit', 'w_' + width, 'q_' + (quality || 'auto')]
  const paramsString = cloudinaryParams.join(',') + '/'
  return `${config.path}${paramsString}${normalizeSrc(src)}`
}
```

where
```
params: ['f_auto', 't_web_ab_2x']
```

title: NextApiRequest should have separate in-path parameters and query arguments.
body:
### Describe the feature you'd like to request

As it is now NextApiRequests have one property **query** and in this property there are mixed up both in-path parameters and query arguments. It would be super helpful I could somehow find out which belong where. This causes huge problems when I try to validate all my requests against OpenAPI yaml files. It is basically impossible to use both at the same time.

For example when FE makes axios call to `/api/user/12345/posts?limit=25`.
and my structure looks like this:
```
pages
-api
--user
---[id]
----posts.ts
```

then I get **query** object like this one `{ id: '12345', limit: '25' }`


### Describe the solution you'd like

I propose separate properties for both parameters and query arguments.

so when I have a NextApiRequest I would be able to simply deconstruct it:

`const { query, params } = request;`

and would get `{ limit: '25' } `and `{ id: '12345' }`

### Describe alternatives you've considered

property query would have objects instead of values that would describe parameters and query arguments.

```
{
  id: {
    value: '12345',
    type: 'param',
  },
  limit: {
    value: '25',
    type: 'query',
  },
}
```

author: rahXephonz

title: Rewrites - any way to limit to one match?
body:
Is there any way to stop processing rewrite rules/patterns, upon the first match?
The default behavior keeps processing the subsequent rewrite patterns, based on the rewritten destination URL.

For example if I have the following rewrite rules:

```
{
  source: '/hotels/',
  destination: '/hotels/overview/?target=world&rule=17&rule17=true'
},
{
  source: '/:country/:market/',
  destination: '/hotels/listing/?country=:country&market=:market&rule=13&rule13=true'
}
```

When I then access /hotels/, it rewrites the URL correctly based on rule 17.
However, after the rewrite the new destination is now matching the pattern of rule 13 and hence gets rewritten again.
This results in a hybrid URL based on both rules, like this:

```/hotels/listing/?target=world&rule=13&rule17=true&country=hotels&market=overview&rule13=true```

I am hoping that there is an obvious fix that I am just missing somehow, so any input or suggestions would be much appreciated.
author: sunech

title: Default locale optimization for On-Demand Revalidate.
body:
### Describe the feature you'd like to request

Following the official localization config, on build default locale files are generated in two places.
E.g. default locale `en`, is deployed on root directory `/` and `/en` directory.

When using the On Demand Revalidate Beta. `unstable_revalidate()` has to be called twice to replace both the file in root and `/en` directory.

I spot this problem when i first reloaded the page it initally gets the file from root or in directory, not sure. Then when i use react routes it gets the other file. 

### Describe the solution you'd like

1. Declare a standard how to revalidate the default locale paths either: `/en/contact` or `/contact`
2. Then revalidate automatically copy the generated file to the two destinations, root `/` and directory `/en`

### Describe alternatives you've considered

First i check if default locale is being revalidated.
Then i call the `unstable_revalidate()` twice with locale on it and the other without locale, e.g `/contact` and `/en/contact`.
That might cause the same data to be fetched twice, also the revalidate process 2x longer.

title: vscode unreliable breakpoints in async fn
body:
Hello,

I'm using vscode with fullstack launch config from doc (https://nextjs.org/docs/advanced-features/debugging#debugging-with-vs-code) for debugging.

I've noticed that breakpoints are not reliable when placed in async functions (client side):

![nextnotworks](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/5154014/157021503-35fa0d51-0e4e-4158-b7de-6d79b649b79b.jpg)
![nextnotworksdebugbrp](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/5154014/157021760-e78c2db1-2aa2-4225-ade8-3caa43cfdc2b.jpg)
![nextdoesnotworkskipped](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/5154014/157021749-5420b2b8-a2db-430a-bcb6-aba2f51fd3d1.jpg)

but totaly reliable in sync ones, eg. placed inside promise then:

![nextworks](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/5154014/157021561-30c1f330-2368-48bd-b10b-12029e95f21e.jpg)
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/5154014/157031730-2ea6356c-ca4d-4854-9ab4-6a392ef7c0ad.png)

When I use async keyword then breakpoints become unreliable:

- They don't hit
- they are placed elsewhere than clicked
- file with transpilled code ("skipped by smartstep") is opened instead of source one
- stepping highlights wrong lines
- muted "unbound breakpoint" is often showed instead of red bound.
- vscode's Debug breakpoint command starts showing not all green checks, but also red "We sent the breakpoint, but it didn't bind to any locations. If this is unexpected:"

I'm suspecting regenerator-runtime (https://www.npmjs.com/package/regenerator-runtime) to be an issue since it's mentioned in transpilled code and I've had already encountered similar breakpoint issue in another non-nextjs project and solved it by rising target ES version and getting rid of regenerator-runtime which is some sort of async/await polyfill? I believe that it somehow breaks sourcemaps.

Is this normal? Do you encounter same issue? Is there some way to solve this or should I just not use async/await?
author: mataslib

title: `revalidate`'s `Cache-Control` values
body:
So I just tried to use Incremental Static Regeneration because it is a nice fit to our use-case: we have an internal CMS that gets updated only once a few days. To speed up the page we set `revalidate: 3600`. We use CloudFront as our CDN. However, after few attempts to verify that the page actually gets refreshed in 1 hour I noticed that Next.js sets `Cache-Control: s-maxage: 3600, stale-while-revalidate` header. This is very problematic if our server sits behind CloudFront, because now we have 2 layers of cache, each respecting `stale-while-revalidate` directive. So request coming after 1h gets old response from CF and CF requests new content in background. Next.js returns old content and refreshes its cache. After another hour CF still returns and old content, but in the background it finally gets a new content from Next.js, although if that content has changed again it still displays outdated one. The cycle never ends.

I don't understand why Next.js does that proactively without ability to opt-out. Nobody else uses CDN except for me?

I tried to use On-demand Revalidation as well, but that one is even crazier - a page with `getStaticProps` without `revalidate` key gets `Cache-Control: s-maxage=31536000, stale-while-revalidate` - how Next.js can even think about setting such header to a page url that obviously doesn't change in between builds? Anyone who accesses the page will forever see the old version of it, no matter how many releases behind.

Am I missing something extremely important here? Because I have a feeling that Next.js can't be that counter-intuitive.

Basically any scenario where the developer actually cares to present the image in the optimal size. Take www.apple.com for example. In Desktop mode: ![hero_iphone_13pro__dhnsegysa42u_largetall_2x](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/1417019/157050418-425ea98f-de58-47e5-98b1-d12757c48ed0.jpg) (hero_iphone_13pro__large_1x, hero_iphone_13pro__large_2x) In Mobile mode: ![hero_iphone_13pro__dhnsegysa42u_mediumtall_2x](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/1417019/157050455-95527335-9274-45d1-af9f-d8e1be413520.jpg) (hero_iphone_13pro__mediumtall_1x, hero_iphone_13pro__mediumtall_2x) In plain old HTML this is as easy as: ``` ``` But with the NextJS Image component... I've spent a whole day trying to use the NextJS Image component for this purpose, and concluded that it simply isn't possible. The component has zero functionality to support this, in my opinion, baseline use-case for a web image component. Not to mention the series of layout issues that present themselves trying to use it for even basic, non-retina images. If it is impossible to achieve crisp artwork while using this, what is the point of the component at all? This has easily been my worst experience trying to develop with NextJS so far. In my opinion, the Image component is over-engineered without getting the fundamentals right. I'd be happy to be proven wrong.

title: next start: Port is opened even if the server cannot be started
body:
### Verify canary release

- [X] I verified that the issue exists in Next.js canary release

### Provide environment information

    Operating System:
      Platform: darwin
      Arch: arm64
      Version: Darwin Kernel Version 21.3.0: Wed Jan  5 21:37:58 PST 2022; root:xnu-8019.80.24~20/RELEASE_ARM64_T6000
    Binaries:
      Node: 16.13.1
      npm: 8.1.2
      Yarn: 1.22.15
      pnpm: 6.11.0
    Relevant packages:
      next: 12.1.1-canary.6
      react: 17.0.2
      react-dom: 17.0.2

### What browser are you using? (if relevant)

_No response_

### How are you deploying your application? (if relevant)

next start

### Describe the Bug

When starting a Next.js server using `next start`, it immediately opens the port. Even if the server crashes right after opening the port, for example due to an error inside `next.config.js`. It always first opens the port, does some initialization (which takes a few seconds based on the app size), then crashes.

This behavior causes some PaaS and Infrastructure-Setups to assume the server is ready and healthy, because they only check if the port was opened. This can cause unhealthy and crashing servers being deployed.

The above scenario with an error inside `next.config.js` happened to me inside a dockerized environment. Inside the `next.config.js`, a file was being required which was available inside the build layer, but not in the final image. This caused the image to build fine, but the server crashed a few seconds after starting the image. Because the next server opened the port immediately and then crashed, the PaaS (GCP CloudRun) did not catch the unhealthy container and it got deployed to production. The PaaS then continued to restart this container again and again, because the opened port indicated that the server started in a healthy state, which it didn't.
If the port would not have been opened, the unhealthy image would not have gotten deployed.

### Expected Behavior

The port should only be opened when the server started in a healthy and ready state.

### To Reproduce

1. Create a new app: `npx create-next-app@latest`
2. Add `require('./bad.js')` to the top of next.config.js
3. Create an empty file `./bad.js`
4. Run `npm run build`  -> build successful
5. Delete `./bad.js`
6. Run `npm start`

Output:

```
ready - started server on 0.0.0.0:3000, url: http://localhost:3000
error - Failed to load next.config.js, see more info here https://nextjs.org/docs/messages/next-config-error
Error: Cannot find module './bad.js'
```

I confirmed that TCP Port 3000 was open for a short amount of time.

title: Translation of react-intl when using values
body:
### Verify canary release

- [X] I verified that the issue exists in Next.js canary release

### Provide environment information

    Operating System:
      Platform: darwin
      Arch: x64
      Version: Darwin Kernel Version 21.3.0: Wed Jan  5 21:37:58 PST 2022; root:xnu-8019.80.24~20/RELEASE_X86_64
    Binaries:
      Node: 14.15.0
      npm: 6.14.8
      Yarn: 1.22.17
      pnpm: N/A
    Relevant packages:
      next: 12.1.1-canary.7
      react: 17.0.2
      react-dom: 17.0.2

### Which example does this report relate to?

with-react-intl

### What browser are you using? (if relevant)

_No response_

### How are you deploying your application? (if relevant)

_No response_

### Describe the Bug

When using values in combination with react-intl/formatjs for example:

```js
<FormattedMessage defaultMessage="Time on site: {seconds}" values={{ seconds: 0 }}/>
// or
intl.formatMessage({ defaultMessage: "Time on site: {seconds}" }, { seconds: 0 });
```

The string gets always translated in the defaultMessage, even though translations exists.
Even when looking in the context of `react-intl` the message is correctly provided and even translated!

```js
const intl = useIntl()
console.log(intl.messages.TbqYJ9) // "Zeit auf dieser Seite: {seconds}"
console.log(intl.formatMessage({ id "TbqYJ9"}, { seconds: 0}); // Zeit auf dieser Seite: 0
```

Then i turned the javascript off - the Static Page gets translated correctly. So that means that the react-intl provider has somthing to do with it i reckon:

```js
export default function MyApp({ Component, pageProps }: AppProps) {
  const { locale, defaultLocale } = useRouter()
  return (
    <IntlProvider
      locale={locale}
      defaultLocale={defaultLocale}
      messages={pageProps.intlMessages}
    >
      <Component {...pageProps} />
    </IntlProvider>
  )
}
```

The funny thing is that it works when working with `yarn dev` - as soon as you build the project, the javascript translation does not work anymore.

### Expected Behavior

When switching to another language the strings should be translated in the different language

### To Reproduce

I created a repository to demonstrate the issue: https://github.com/ChiefORZ/react-intl-nextjs-production-bug

```sh
git clone https://github.com/ChiefORZ/react-intl-nextjs-production-bug
cd react-intl-nextjs-production-bug
yarn
yarn build
yarn start
```

Then open your browser at localhost:3000 and switch to German or French - the string *Time on site: {seconds}* will not be translated, but the rest of the site will be respectively

title: CSS-in-JS solution that supports SWC and PostCSS?
body:
Are there any CSS-in-JS solutions that work with SWC and support PostCSS? As far as I know, the only CSS-in-JS libraries that work with SWC are styled-jsx and styled-components. 

styled-jsx supported PostCSS via babel plugins, but [I don't know if there's a plan to support it in SWC](https://github.com/vercel/styled-jsx/issues/782). I don't think styled-components ever had official support for PostCSS, so I don't have much hope there.

      <meta property="og:image" content={image} key="og-image" />

      <meta name="twitter:title" content={title} key="tw-title"/>
      <meta name="twitter:description" content={description} key="tw-desc"/>
      <meta name="twitter:image" content={image} key="tw-image"/>
      <meta name="twitter:card" content="summary_large_image" key="tw-card"/>
</Head>
```

Result:

Summary: things work as expected in the browser but facebook debugger is not picking up the component specific changes.

In the browser I can see that the correct metadata is loaded for one.js (presenting the component specific overrides) and two.js (presenting the metadata from _app.js). However the facebook debugger is only picking up metadata from _app.js not from one.js - so all pages have the same metadata. 


### Expected Behavior

Both the facebook debugger and the client browser to load the same metadata. Facebook debugger is somehow not collecting the page specific metadata overrides.

### To Reproduce

I have a live site running here:

https://next18.syntapse.co.uk/two doesnt define its own metadata so loads default <meta> tags from _app.js using keys to avoid duplication

https//next18.syntapse.co.uk/one loads overrided <meta> tags using keys to avoid duplication

This works as expected in the browser. Load this into a browser and note the differences in metadata. Note the metadata changes from the default to the override. 

Load this into the facebook debugger and note that /one loads the same metadata as /two and also / url.
