title: Use plain scss -> bundle it to css -> reference css in components?
body:
### Heres my idea...

Seeing as components can only import css files and not sass files I want to edit the webpack config to have similar functionality to [nuxt ](https://nuxtjs.org/docs/configuration-glossary/configuration-build/#extractcss)where it exports scss files to separate css files.

I would import a sass or many sass files into `_app.js` or tell webpack where to find my sass files and webpack would export those sass files into separate css files or it would know any scss files without underscores should be its own file.

Then i would like to reference that css file in my components so i have a normal sass implementation. This way I can control what gets bundled into the main scss file and what gets split into its own file - i have control of my css splitting and I get to use plain sass.

### Why not use css modules or css in js?

1. CSS modules is not all that, i have used it extensively and it has its bugbears, so i switched to css in js.
2. Styled Components or CSS-in-JS might decrease css bundle size but it increases page load time because js has to load styles into the head and js is blocking causing some of my page load times to be longer.
3. In a world where the difference of 1s on your load time means making the cut or not and developer experience matters, its come down to this for me. I cannot risk the css in js penalties and the nuances of css modules. I am seeking for a more standard scss implementation.

Please comment here if you have any ideas! Thanks

author: LNFWebsite

title: Cache headers for 404's in ISR/dynamic pages
body:
Hello 👋 

I'm working on an application which has various dynamic routes that are built with ISR, with a blocking fallback. Essentially this means that pages could, and should be published from a CMS while the application is running. This works super nice - for the most part. An issue which I've found seems to be the cache headers that are given when the page hasn't been published yet and are visited, then published afterwards. 

Upon not finding any results (`404`) the response has a  cache header `Cache-Control: s-maxage=31536000, stale-while-revalidate` - Which would make a lot of sense if the pages were static. 
However if a user navigates to a specific path before the page is published, it is cached as a `404` for a very long time, while in reality it could be published any minute!  I expected the cache behaviour to be similar to the one of the hits - Cached for the same amount as the revalidation interval.

Similarly if using a `next/link` with prefetch, the 404 on a missing `.json` file corresponding to a missing page in `_next/data/<buildId>/` is also cached. This would however be resolved with a rebuild - as the `buildId` part changes and effectively invalidates the cache.

This issue is even harder to work around when using an external CDN which cache these results based on the cache headers provided from the original request. For each published page i have to invalidate the CDN cache manually, in case it has been cached as a `404` previously - and thus is cached for a whole year.


I'm wondering if there is some way of overriding the cache headers for dynamic pages (which should be the same as the other revaldation-intervals), and potentially within folders for the prefetch-data, to avoid a year-long `404` being cached. 

Perhaps there's something i've missed in the documentation 😬

author: marceloverdijk

title: next/image transformations prop
body:
Those of us working with the cloudinary loader and next images need a way to apply transformations to the image url. Transformations involving quality and image size are applied already based on other props, but there's no way to include additional values.

For example, the source assets I work with on cloudinary have transparent backgrounds. Adding a background colour to the url can reduce the download size as cloudinary will generate and serve a jpg instead of a png.

I first mentioned this issue in https://github.com/vercel/next.js/discussions/34268.

I propose a `transformations` prop for next image which accepts an object for the Image component that accepts an object keyed with supported [transformation](https://cloudinary.com/documentation/transformation_reference) values (`b` for background, `d` for default image etc.) and accepts string values. This object will be merged with the existing transformations.

```jsx
transformations={{ b: 'rgb:ffffff' }} //=> "[host]/q_10,w_200,b_rgb:ffffff/[imagePath]"
```

I do not know how this will work with other image loaders, but I assume they will need a way to apply transformations too, if they support them.
author: jacknevitt

title: Static video imports
body:
### Describe the feature you'd like to request

Like how it's current possibly to [statically import `.png`, `.jpg` and `.webp` images](https://nextjs.org/docs/basic-features/image-optimization#local-images):

```js
import profilePic from '../public/me.png'
// => gets a { src: string, height: number, width: number, blurDataURL?: string }
```

it'd be nice if we could also import videos to get a similar object providing at least a URL to the video.ge

### Describe the solution you'd like

e.g. being able to do

```js
import animation from '../public/animation.webm'
// => at least { src: string }, possibly also including with and height
```

### Describe alternatives you've considered

A full `<Video>` component like the `<Image>` component could be useful as well, but would require a lot more processing, is presumably harder to implement, and doesn't currently support statically exported sites. (There's also https://github.com/vercel/next.js/discussions/20323 and https://github.com/vercel/next.js/discussions/26388 for that.)

We _can_ currently place the video files in `/public/` and manually write the link to it, but then we don't get warnings at build time when those links break, and doesn't allow us to colocate a video and the component it's used in.

title: Referrer Header Validation for prevent vulnerability CSRF
body:
### Describe the feature you'd like to request

594 / 5.000
Resultados de traducción
Hello how are you.
I am investigating how to validate the referrer header of a resource (image, css, js, etc) in nextjs and prevent them from being loaded when the URL is not allowed, in short, to validate the URLs through an allowed list of URLs.
I didn't find any info yet other than using a custom nextjs server, but according to their documentation this has static resource optimization issues.
I mention this because in a pentest report of my work, they indicated a CSRF vulnerability because the application in nextjs does not validate the header referer of the resources. 

### Describe the solution you'd like

I would like to be able to set in the next.config.js an allowed list of urls for the referrer header

### Describe alternatives you've considered

Currently I read that I can validate the resource referer header using a custom next.js server, however this brings performance issues according to its own documentation.

title: Support optional chaining by default
body:
### Describe the feature you'd like to request

I have had many **can't access property of undefined** errors and I could figure out why, although I have been using optional chaining.  After some search I found the reason and also the solution. I believe many people are affected by this, and are probably losing their minds like I was, because they can't figure out why this is happening :(

I know this is already a known bug but I would like to propose adding Babel's solution for this _by default_, as explained here: [@babel/plugin-proposal-optional-chaining](https://babeljs.io/docs/en/babel-plugin-proposal-optional-chaining)

I don't know if you already are aware of this, but didn't do it because it may affect something else. However I noticed this and thought it would be beneficial to be added. If I'm wrong just ignore this 👍 


### Describe the solution you'd like

It's just a matter of installing the package provided by Babel, like so: 

```
npm install --save-dev @babel/plugin-proposal-optional-chaining
```

and then adding this code in a **.babelrc** config file
```
{
  "plugins": ["@babel/plugin-proposal-optional-chaining"]
}
```

### Describe alternatives you've considered

That would already be all :D

title: How run next.js with node-sharp for docker
body:
**I have a problem implementing sharp for Dockerfile.** 

**Error: 'sharp' is required to be installed in standalone mode for the image optimization to function correctly**

Next.js with sharp works fine for local developing:
 - next 12.0.1
 - sharp 0.30.2
 - node 16.xx
 - npm 8.xx
 - OS - macOS Monterey - 12.2.1, M1 PRO

**next.config.js**

    module.exports = {
      experimental: {
        outputStandalone: true,
      },

}

**Dockerfile:**

    FROM node:16-alpine AS deps
    # Check https://github.com/nodejs/docker-node/tree/b4117f9333da4138b03a546ec926ef50a31506c3#nodealpine to understand why libc6-compat might be needed.
    RUN apk add --no-cache libc6-compat
    
    WORKDIR /app
    COPY package.json package-lock.json ./ 
    RUN npm ci
    
    # Rebuild the source code only when needed
    FROM node:16-alpine AS builder
    WORKDIR /app
    COPY --from=deps /app/node_modules ./node_modules
    COPY . .
    
    # Next.js collects completely anonymous telemetry data about general usage.
    # Learn more here: https://nextjs.org/telemetry
    # Uncomment the following line in case you want to disable telemetry during the build.
    # ENV NEXT_TELEMETRY_DISABLED 1
    
    RUN npm run build
    
    # Production image, copy all the files and run next
    FROM node:16-alpine AS runner
    WORKDIR /app
    
    ENV NODE_ENV production
    # Uncomment the following line in case you want to disable telemetry during runtime.
    # ENV NEXT_TELEMETRY_DISABLED 1
    
    RUN addgroup --system --gid 1001 nodejs
    RUN adduser --system --uid 1001 nextjs
    
    # You only need to copy next.config.js if you are NOT using the default configuration
    COPY --from=builder /app/public ./public
    COPY --from=builder /app/package.json ./package.json
    COPY --from=builder /app/package.json ./package.json
    COPY --from=builder /app/next.config.js ./
    COPY --from=builder /app/next-i18next.config.js ./
    COPY --from=builder /app/next-sitemap.js ./cd
    COPY --from=builder /app/jsconfig.json ./jsconfig.json
    COPY --from=builder /app/data/ ./data
    COPY --from=builder /app/components/ ./components
    COPY --from=builder /app/utils/ ./utils
    COPY --from=builder /app/assets/ ./assets
    
    # Automatically leverage output traces to reduce image size 
    # https://nextjs.org/docs/advanced-features/output-file-tracing
    COPY --from=builder --chown=nextjs:nodejs /app/.next/standalone ./
    COPY --from=builder --chown=nextjs:nodejs /app/.next/static ./.next/static
    
    USER nextjs
    
    EXPOSE 3000
    
    ENV PORT 3000
    
    CMD ["node", "server.js"]

**.env file:**

    NEXT_SHARP_PATH=/tmp/node_modules/sharp next start

Sharp is installed in package.json

I checked both Next/Vercel tuts:
- [https://nextjs.org/docs/messages/install-sharp][1]
- [https://nextjs.org/docs/messages/sharp-missing-in-production][2]

**RUN Docker:** 

    docker build --no-cache . -t website-app && docker run --name website -p 3000:3000 website-app


  [1]: https://nextjs.org/docs/messages/install-sharp
  [2]: https://nextjs.org/docs/messages/sharp-missing-in-production

title: outputStandalone flag makes a /tmp dir in a weird path
body:
Hello,

We want to start using the experimental flag: outputStandalone, and while testing this out we found a small issue.

When running `npm run build` on our Jenkins with `outputStandalone` flag on `true`, we get this error message from npm at the end of build:
```
> Build error occurred
[Error: EACCES: permission denied, mkdir '/var/lib/tmp'] {
  errno: -13,
  code: 'EACCES',
  syscall: 'mkdir',
  path: '/var/lib/tmp'
}
```

We can't understand why it wants to create a tmp folder on such random path (which is of course protected on our Jenkins server).

We also double checked our npm config to see where the tmp dir is:
```
$ npm config ls -l | grep tmp
tmp = "/tmp"
```

So we do not understand why outputStandalone creates a tmp dir at such path, is this a bug or are we doing something wrong?

title: Support _app directory to act as _app.js
body:
### Describe the feature you'd like to request

Organizing files of `pages` inside directories helps. The problem is that `_app` can't be a directory. So you end up with this situation

```
.
└── pages/
    ├── somePage/
    │   ├── index.js
    │   ├── somePageComponent1.js
    │   ├── somePageComponent2.js
    │   └── etc.js
    ├── anotherPage/
    │   ├── index.js
    │   └── ...etc
    ├── _app.js
    └── _appComponent(where do I live? 😔).js
```

### Describe the solution you'd like

Ideally, it should be possible to make `_app` a directory so that supporting components can live within it as well, like this

```
.
└── pages/
    ├── somePage/
    │   ├── index.js
    │   ├── somePageComponent1.js
    │   ├── somePageComponent2.js
    │   └── etc.js
    ├── anotherPage/
    │   ├── index.js
    │   └── ...etc
    └── _app/
        ├── index.js
        └── _appComponent(This is my home 😊).js
```

### Describe alternatives you've considered

Have an `_app` directory that `_app.js` imports from.

title: Preflight cache for middleware should behave the same in dev and prod
body:
### Verify canary release

- [X] I verified that the issue exists in Next.js canary release

### Provide environment information

    Operating System:
      Platform: win32
      Arch: x64
      Version: Windows 10 Enterprise
    Binaries:
      Node: 16.14.0
      npm: 8.3.1
      Yarn: 1.22.15
      pnpm: N/A
    Relevant packages:
      next: 12.1.0
      react: 17.0.2
      react-dom: 17.0.2

### What browser are you using? (if relevant)

_No response_

### How are you deploying your application? (if relevant)

Vercel

### Describe the Bug

In developement I added a middleware to "gate" a set of pages. While in development the middleware is call on every route change and the functionnality is working well.

Once deployed in production on Vercel the functionnality is not working anymore because the middleware is not called anymore on every route change.

I believe it's because of the preflight cache (view https://github.com/vercel/next.js/pull/32767).

It's confusing that the middleware doesn't behave the same in development and in production. It's very error prone.

To be honest I am not fully understanding why the preflight cache exist so my proposition might be silly. From a developer standpoint this functionnality doesn't makes me feels confident about my code :)

### Expected Behavior

The middleware preflich cache behave the same in development and in production.

### To Reproduce

- Have a middleware at the root of /pages with a console log everytime it's called
- Start the app with a production build
- Navigate between the pages

The console will not log on subsequent route changes.

title: Better monitoring / Improvements in nextjs server stdout (logs) output
body:
### Describe the feature you'd like to request

Motivation:
Better nextjs logging (especially for unhandled exceptions) to understand exactly context of possible errors/warnings and be able to respond very fast to those situations.

Hi, right now after `next build` when we start next server with `next start` we see output logs only if error happens.

I would like to be able to have more control here over
1. log format. Example print json output instead of line by line example that i copy below
```
Error: {"size":0,"timeout":0}
at getStaticProps (/usr/src/app/.next/server/pages/post/[id].js:3576:11)
at runMicrotasks (<anonymous>)
```
2. Ability to add meta/context to nextjs logs after specifying a json format. Example enrich all logs with request url as well, or with custom tracing field whatever developer wants.
In that case above log would look like {"message": "some message here", "url": "domain.com/someurl", "appVersion":"something", ...othermeta}
3. In above real log that I posted i see js file name, but my code is in typescript. How can we apply source maps so see ts file line  in log output in those unhandled errors ?

PS: This is not about wrapping everything in a try catch and logging to custom logger. We are doing that sending to newrelic, but many things happen and are not handled. In those cases next server prints the error but without formatting context and is not very useful, therefore my suggestion to having a great way like in other frameworks for full visibility



### Describe the solution you'd like

As described in feature request I want to be able to do those actions.
I think one way maybe can be configurations in next.config.js but as I don't know nextjs internals well I am open to any solution that is best fit

### Describe alternatives you've considered

Unfortunately nothing because is impossible currently to add meta and format next output to json.

author: mengtongun

title: Feature flags and bundling of pages and components
body:
Hello,

I am trying to find a robust way to implement a feature flag system for our projects. We have a single repository that is shared among many clients and has a lot of configurations that map to features that some clients will have, others might not have. Currently we still see too much of our "behind a flag" code being bundled and also we have entire pages that would ideally be removed from bundling..did anyone solve this in a convenient way?

What we want to ultimately achieve is that we can turn on and off many of these features (not always entire pages, could also be just normal components inside an existing page) and have that not affect other parts of the site. Meaning that if a feature is turned off that will actually remove as much as possible the code for that feature from the bundle, ideally also maybe even remove an entire page.


Our current approach / idea to tackle this is as following (we are not yet doing it quite like this): 

Server rendered / entire pages 

We basically check the flag and return a 404 inside `getServerSideProps`, still the whole render is being compiled so it is still somewhat there and parts of it (if they use code that is shared with other pages) might even end up in the "common chunk" -> 👎

This could mean that if we have a lot of these pages we might inadvertently still be making the bundle worse with features that might not be used on some sites. Those sites would still be penalized in terms of performance for a feature they are not using.

Client side / Individual components

We check the flag in the component and render `null`. This however still bundles also the component see this issue we have opened https://github.com/vercel/next.js/discussions/34090 ..meaning the whole code is actually still there 👎 We can use dynamic imports for these components and then we mitigate this situation👍


I wonder if anyone has good feedback on such types of strategies to be able to have turned off features without penalizing the build and the overall site performance?
author: joaogarin

title: Allow next/jest to transform selected node_modules
body:
### Describe the feature you'd like to request

The next/jest config prevents any node_modules from being added to the transform list https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/acbd54322f447caaee09b1262e4687434ea0268c/packages/next/build/jest/jest.ts#L118

Some node_modules really do need transforming, e.g. https://github.com/sindresorhus/serialize-error/blob/main/index.js . This is a common enough pattern that it is explicitly mentioned in the Jest config docs https://jestjs.io/docs/configuration#transformignorepatterns-arraystring

Please allow the custom Jest config to override the next/jest assumptions

### Describe the solution you'd like

don't ignore custom transformIgnorePatterns like

```js
const customJestConfig = {
  transformIgnorePatterns: ["/node_modules/(?!(serialize-error)/)"],
};
```
it's a slightly tricky one, it might require a regex to detect node_modules matching patterns, or a separate config option in the `nextJest({...options})` invocation, e.g. `{transformNodeModules:["serialize-error"],}`.



### Describe alternatives you've considered

Setting up Jest manually

author: tettoffensive

title: SSG - Fallback Mode to CSR Dynamic Routes Not Generated During Build
body:
### Describe the feature you'd like to request

When serving a Next app from the output of `next export` (using Next.js in SSG mode), I would like to be able to use `next/router` to route to dynamic routes that were not generated during the static build and to CSR those pages instead.

As a companion feature, the `/404` route will detect when the `asPath` matches the dynamic route and redirect the user there. Assuming the site operator has correctly configured fallback routing, this redirect would ensure users are automatically directed to the correct page with no additional Next.js configuration.

#### Use case

For a statically hosted Next.js app, use dynamic routing to display pages from a data set that changes too frequently to be integrated with the build process.

### Describe the solution you'd like

First, a new fallback mode should be introduced that enables the desired feature. When in the new fallback mode two potential strategies may implement the feature.

When routing to the dynamic path client side, either:
1. `getStaticProps` is run, and Its output is used to render the page client side.
2. A new page file static function export, let's call it `getPropsClientSide`, is run. This new function's output is merged into the `pageProps` returned by `getStaticProps` during the build process. The merged object is used to render the page client side.


### Describe alternatives you've considered

There is no way I am aware of that this can be achieved using out-of-the-box dynamic routing/path parameters in Next.js. If there is, I apologize for this feature request, but would ask that the documentation be updated to explain the strategy.

The only alternative is to forego path parameters and use query string parameters, or "clever" solutions like implementing CDN redirect rules and mock path parameters using the `as` argument of `router.push`;

title: Ability to override webpack devtool in development mode for detailed sourcemaps
body:
### Describe the feature you'd like to request

Next allows a custom webpack devtool for production but in development, it reverts any custom devtool to `eval-source-map`. It would be great if there is a way to override the default devtool in development mode to allow for more detailed modes such as `source-map`.

### Describe the solution you'd like

Overrides to `webpack.devtool` will be ignored in development mode as per the [documentation](https://nextjs.org/docs/messages/improper-devtool). I think it would be great if this override by next was removed.

### Describe alternatives you've considered

With Next 12, using `zeit/next-source-maps` became deprecated and overriding devtool in `next.config.js` doesn't seem to work. And I couldn't find other alternatives solution after a fairly exhaustive search of the web and the documentation.

title: Consider using RSLint instead of ESLint
body:
### Describe the feature you'd like to request

Hi,

So basically it's nothing new, Next.js already includes built-in linting, which works great, but considering recent changes from JS based tooling to Rust based tooling, I think a huge performance improvement can be made here too.

### Describe the solution you'd like

My proposal is to replace ESLint with RSLint, which is a linter written in Rust and it is 8x faster on a rather small 11k LOC project (0.05 sec vs 0.4 sec), and an incredible 113x times faster on a medium 42k LOC(0.4 sec vs 47 seconds)!.
I can only assume the performance difference are even crazier the larger the project is.


### Describe alternatives you've considered

Well, none.
The best alternative is ESLint which you already use.

The main problem I can see while implementing RSLint is writing the custom rules you wrote for ESLint again, but this time in Rust.

title: Runtime variables for static generation
body:
I know that runtimeVariables do not work with static generation because those pages are precompiled (mentioned here: https://github.com/vercel/next.js/discussions/11493#discussioncomment-14606)
Looking around, I found about Incremental Static Generation. Actually, we can define a revalidate timeout in our getStaticProps method.
In that case, the runtimeVariables are picked up when the page is recompiled.

Is there any way to tell NextJS to "regenerate" a static page only once? In that case, my page could be compiled on my production environment only once on first access. After that, it will inherit my production runtime variable, and I could reuse that page for all requests. I dont need to revalidate the page after X seconds. I just need to compile it once on first access (no need to compile it on build stage).

Is there any way of doing that?, or it should be a feature request?

Thanks

title: Standalone build for monorepos
body:
I know this has been brought up a few times, but I ran into some weird issues with the standalone build feature in a monorepo.

I couldn't get it to work without a few changes. My app package is called `dashboard` here. I'm using Yarn v1 workspaces.

```
  experimental: {

```
# Automatically leverage output traces to reduce image size 
# https://nextjs.org/docs/advanced-features/output-file-tracing
COPY --chown=nextjs:nodejs /packages/dashboard/.next/standalone ./

COPY /packages/dashboard/public ./packages/dashboard/public
# These are partially installed for some reason. Remove or it won't work.
RUN rm -rf ./packages/dashboard/node_modules/react
RUN rm -rf ./packages/dashboard/node_modules/next

...

CMD ["node", "./packages/dashboard/server.js"]
```

Basically, I had to move everything into the `standalone/packages/dashboard` directory, and use the server.js there.

If I tried to move server.js to root, it said it couldn't find a production next build.
If I didn't move the static files into the packages, I was getting 404s for every chunk.
For some reason I had partially built dependencies in the packages directory that I had to remove. I should probably move these to the root package.json, but I'm not sure if this was something else.

So yeah, this was weird. Am I missing something? Is this something solvable in documentation or directly in next.js?

author: samueldusek

title: Retrieve the rendered route in Middleware
body:
### Describe the feature you'd like to request

Part of our company's logging standard is that we would log the matched route. This is used to group requests. For instance, as part of a structured log we would log something like:
```
{
  message: "http request"
  path: "/user/1234",
  route: "/user/[userid]"
}
```
While it is possible to retrieve the `path` in middleware, it's currently not possible to get the matched route.

### Describe the solution you'd like

Expose the matched route to middleware, preferablly on request, but on response would be acceptable. I realize this may mean compiling the routing logic and shipping to the edge to match the route there. Or I suppose it could mean returning the route as part of a response header from Next.js?

### Describe alternatives you've considered

Currently the only way I can think to do this is by implementing a Custom Server or by only logging `getServerSideProps()` calls. Both have significant drawbacks. Annoyingly, in `getServerSideProps()` you don't have access to the route and a Custom Server you have to call Next.js routing logic yourself. :/

title: Support dynamic routes in static export, with traditional SPA-like routing
body:
### Describe the feature you'd like to request

next.js should support a static export where `getStaticPaths` is not defined but dynamic routes still work, just like in a traditional SPA: the server always serves the same index.html entrypoint, but routing is done on the client side.

### Describe the solution you'd like

Users would configure their webserver to serve `index.html` file for every path, and routing would happen just like it typically does on SPAs. on the front-end. For example:
* app contains `pages/user/[id].tsx` (user page)
* build static export, which generates `_next/static/chunks/pages/user/[id].js` and a single entry-point, `index.html`
* browser requests `GET /user/foo`, webserver hosting the static export responds with the contents of `index.html`
* the client-side code in parses the `/user/foo` route, loads `_next/static/chunks/pages/user/[id].js` and renders the user page.

Note that this already *almost* works. If you configure your webserver to serve the right files (e.g. `/users/[id.html]` for requests to `/users/<id>`) then the app works entirely as expected, including all client-side routing.

### Describe alternatives you've considered

Not using next.js, but this would be a shame! Even though none of my content is static, next.js still offers a great development environment with fast TS and CSS-in-JS compilation and bundling, code splitting, and routing.

author: zhangcx93

title: Request for feedback: Image Component ‘Raw’ layout
body:
Starting with version 12.1.1-canary.11, I’ve added experimental support for [a new image component mode layout mode called `“raw”`](https://nextjs.org/docs/api-reference/next/image#layout).  This mode removes the spacers and styling associated with next/image’s responsive behavior, rendering a “raw” image element with no styling. The component still requires height and width information in order to prevent layout shift, and will pass those attributes to the underlying image unless you also provide a `sizes` attribute, which indicates that the image is expected to behave responsively.

I have also enabled the use of the `style` attribute in all image modes to pass style properties directly to the underlying image element. Be aware that in layout modes other than `”raw”`, any styling required for that layout mode will take precedence over provided styling, if there’s a conflict.

I would like to gather feedback on the new `”raw”` layout mode before we move it out of experimental. I encourage anyone who’s had trouble getting the `next/image` component styled the way they want to try the experimental `”raw”` layout and tell me how it worked. 

[See this section in the documentation for how to enable the experimental layout mode.](https://nextjs.org/docs/api-reference/next/image#experimental-raw-layout-mode)

***

Here’s an example template for responses, though any feedback is appreciated:

1) What layout / styling behavior were you previously unable to accomplish with `next/image`?
2) What layout modes did you try?
3) Did the experimental `”raw”` layout mode solve your problem? 	- If not, what changes would allow it to solve the problem?
4) Do you have any additional feedback about the new mode or its documentation?

title: Dynamic function can't import sub components.
body:
### Describe the feature you'd like to request

Hi, there. Thanks for this amazing library and the efforts of the contributors. 💪

I can't render sub components by dynamic import. 

When I have a component like this 
```tsx
const Tabs=()=><div/>
const Tab=()=><div/>
Tabs.Tab = Tab
export default Tabs.
```

Usage
```tsx
...
const Tabs = dynamic(import('...')));
render(){
    return <Tabs>
        <Tabs.Tab></Tabs.Tab>
    </Tabs>
}
...
```
This will trigger an error, Because `Tabs.Tab` is undefined.

The relevant code of dynamic library
```
// next.js/packages/next/shared/lib/loadable.js (lines:144)
...
return React.createElement(resolve(state.loaded), props)
...
```

It looks like the mount property on the object has been removed in this step. So, I wonder to have an api that keep properties alive. 

I have seen some UI component library, according to the normal dynamic writing method, this problem still occurs, which is too vague for most people, there are some black boxes in this step, I can provide a PR if the scheme feels good, looking forward to reply.

### Describe the solution you'd like

I think it could be have an API that handles `state.loaded` data, and processing component before returning it.

e.g.
```tsx
...
const Tabs = dynamic(import('...'),{process:(loaded,components)=>{
    components.Tab = loaded.Tab; // I know the reactElement obj is immutable, Is there any other way to do it ?
    return components
}}));
...
```

### Describe alternatives you've considered

```tsx
const Tabs = dynamic(import('....'));
Tabs.Tab = dynamic(import('....').then(r=>r.default.Tab));
```

title: middlewares and postgresql db queries
body:
It seems that middlewares are VERY sensitive to how modules get exported.  I get `Module not found: Can't resolve 'fs'` when trying to implement them.
At first the stack looked like this:
```sh
error - ./node_modules/json-schema-to-typescript/dist/src/index.js:64:0
Module not found: Can't resolve 'fs'

Import trace for requested module:
./src/lib/json/schema/_index.ts
./src/codegen/schema/validations/result.ts
./src/codegen/schema/validations/_index.ts
./src/lib/account/_index.ts
./src/pages/_middleware.ts
```
Fine, something is wrong with my re-exports and `json-schema-to-typescript` probably does something to file system.
Split exports around.

Then I get this:
```sh
error - ./node_modules/nodemailer/lib/dkim/index.js:10:0
Module not found: Can't resolve 'fs'

Import trace for requested module:
./node_modules/nodemailer/lib/mailer/index.js
./node_modules/nodemailer/lib/nodemailer.js
./src/lib/email/_index.ts
./src/lib/account/email.ts
./src/lib/account/_index.ts
./src/pages/_middleware.ts
```
Looks like re-export problem again, now with `nodemailer`.
Split exports around.

The next one is baffling though:
```sh
error - ./node_modules/pg-connection-string/index.js:4:0
Module not found: Can't resolve 'fs'

Import trace for requested module:
./node_modules/pg/lib/connection-parameters.js
./node_modules/pg/lib/client.js
./node_modules/pg/lib/index.js
./node_modules/pg-promise/lib/main.js
./node_modules/pg-promise/lib/index.js
./src/database/lib.ts
./src/database/_index.ts
./src/database/queries/account/_index.ts
./src/lib/account/auth.ts
./src/lib/account/_index.ts
./src/pages/_middleware.ts
```
This one can't be fixed by rearranging the modules, since this stack is for a function which does a database call (the whole purpose of this middleware).
At this point I realized I don't even know what is the problem. Aren't middlewares server-only? Why would having `fs` be a problem?

title: RFC: Client Certificate Authentication
body:
# Goals

- Enable server-side data fetching with [`getServerSideProps()`](https://nextjs.org/docs/basic-features/data-fetching/get-server-side-props) and [`getStaticProps()`](https://nextjs.org/docs/basic-features/data-fetching/get-static-props) to services that require client certificate authentication
- Enable API routes to require client certificate authentication from the browser, and similarly enable API routes to make HTTPS requests to other services that require client certificate authentication
- Minimize the need to manage an HTTPS proxy (e.g. nginx, httpd, Amazon CloudFront, etc.) for incoming requests with self-hosting deployment options

# Background
Some large enterprise companies and government organizations require client certificate authentication (i.e. [two-way TLS](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mutual_authentication#mTLS)) for all internal network traffic.  As Next.js currently exists, the only way a developer can achieve this is by using a custom server and configuring it to use an instance of [https.Server](https://nodejs.org/api/https.html#class-httpsserver) instead.  

The problem is that this opts you out of [Automatic Static Optimization](https://nextjs.org/docs/advanced-features/automatic-static-optimization) and it appears features of the integrated Next router as well:

> Before deciding to use a custom server, please keep in mind that it should only be used when the integrated router of Next.js can't meet your app requirements.

Depending on the network scale and its geographic locations, a user's connection to the network can be quite slow usually as a result from limited bandwidth or high latency.  Getting first class TLS/HTTPS support from Next.js would enable developers building internal systems at these enterprises to deliver next-generation experiences to their users.

# Implementation
I am more than happy to take a shot at implementation, opening a PR, and iterating on an acceptable solution with the Next.js maintainers and community.  I am open to any and all suggestions for how we can achieve this and I figured you all probably know best (assuming I get the greenlight to go ahead and get started 😆).

I was thinking something along the lines of adding an optional `https` object to some part of the [`next.config.js`](https://nextjs.org/docs/api-reference/next.config.js/introduction) that allows for some subset of [`tls.connect()`](https://nodejs.org/api/tls.html#tlsconnectoptions-callback) options to be specified, at a minimum something like this:

```js
// next.config.js
module.exports = {
  https: {
    cert: 'path/to/cert',
    key: 'path/to/key',
    ca: 'path/to/ca'
  }
}
```

We would also allow other acceptable values `string[]` (or even `Buffer`/`Buffer[]` if your config exports a function)  for `cert`, `key`, and `ca`.  Other config options could also be `pfx` and `passphrase` for users who have keystores instead.

The idea would be to then pass along these values to dev/production Next.js servers and if the `https` object exists:

- substitute the `http` module with the `https` module
- pass the values along as `options` to an [`https.Agent`](https://nodejs.org/api/https.html#class-httpsagent)
- pass the values  along as `options` to [`https.createServer`](https://nodejs.org/api/https.html#httpscreateserveroptions-requestlistener)

I'm not sure what makes the most sense in the context of Next, but I am eager to take a stab at this with some guidance and the go-ahead.  It wouldn't let me select the RFC category, but let me know what you think!

title: Errors after ending response in getServerSideProps
body:
Under certain conditions, getServerSideProps, in my app, can fully handle the response, and therefore the page does not need to be rendered.

I am unable to do this in middleware as I need access to native nodejs modules in order to determine if the request can be fulfilled by the page without needing to render.

Using /api is possible (and I do use it in my work around), but the request to the resource is much cleaner without the API path (e.g., /path/to/some/file.txt instead of /api/download/path/to/some/file.txt). My work around involves returning a redirect from my getServerSideProps method to the API URL, but I would much rather not have the redirect.

If I let getServerSideProps handle it (and end the response manually), from the user's perspective, everything works, but the server logs are showing errors of attempting to write the header after the headers have already been sent). I assume this is because I've already written the body and next.js is trying to write headers for the page that it is about to render.

title: How to add a file (robots.txt) outside of basePath?
body:
A common use case for basePath is, for example, to serve all pages in the blog.domain.com app under `/blog`, so you can then access that via www.domain.com/blog. (This is called multi-zone in the docs.)

However, you would also need to add a robots.txt to blog.domain.com to prevent it from being crawled, or else you'll get duplicate content indexed in Google, which is bad for SEO.

Is there a way to add such files outside of the basePath? Currently, if you simply add a public/robots.txt, it'll show up under /blog/robots.txt.

Thanks.

title: Type Error: x is not a function on per page layout
body:
I am following the nextjs [documentation](https://nextjs.org/docs/basic-features/layouts) to implement the per page layout. I have ran into a type error which I don't understand in `_app.tsx`:`TypeError: getLayout is not a function`. I am using the same typing provided in the docs but I have the function extracted in as a helper function.

Code: 

`_app.tsx`
``` typescript
const MyApp: FC<AppPropsWithLayout> = ({ Component, pageProps: { session, ...pageProps } }: AppPropsWithLayout) => {
  const getLayout = Component.getLayout ?? ((page): ReactNode => page)

  return <SessionProvider session={session}>{getLayout(<Component {...pageProps} />)}</SessionProvider>
}

export default MyApp
```
`index.tsx`
``` typescript
const Home: NextPageWithLayout = () => {
  return <Dashboard />
}

Home.getLayout = mainLayout

export default Home
```

`layout.tsx` Helper function
``` typescript
export const mainLayout = (page: ReactElement): ReactNode => {
  return <Layout>{page}</Layout>
}

export const blankLayout = (page: ReactElement): ReactNode => {
  return <BlankLayout>{page}</BlankLayout>
}
```

`types`
``` typescript
export type NextPageWithLayout = NextPage & {
  getLayout?: (page: ReactElement) => ReactNode
}

export type AppPropsWithLayout = AppProps & {
  Component: NextPageWithLayout
}
```

`Error`
```
error - pages/_app.tsx (14:45) @ MyApp
TypeError: getLayout is not a function
  12 |   const getLayout = Component.getLayout ?? ((page): ReactNode => page)
  13 | 
> 14 |   return <SessionProvider session={session}>{getLayout(<Component {...pageProps} />)}</SessionProvider>
     |                                             ^
  15 | }
  16 | 
  17 | export default MyApp
```

title: New `next add` create using template subcommand
body:
## What
In a recent [NuxtJS](https://nuxtjs.org/) release they added a really neat feature where you can invoke `npx nuxt add` to create a new page, component, api route, etc with ease.

It's nothing special but a neat addition that could make creating new pages, components, etc much simpler if some existing templates, preferably with a toggle or auto-detecting TypeScript support, were available to give a boilerplate to get started.

Thoughts & feedback on this as an addition?
Do others think this is worth adding or is it not worth it & better to leave it up to others to create their own boilerplate / refer to `examples/`

## Examples

```zsh
# Create a new `pages/posts/[id].jsx` page, or `pages/posts/[id].tsx` if TypeScript is detected
npx next add page posts/[id]
```

```zsh
# Create a new `Header.jsx` component, or `Header.tsx` if TypeScript is detected
npx next add component Header
```

```zsh
# Create a new `api/graphql.js` route, or `api/graphql.ts` route if TypeScript is detected
npx next add api graphql
```

## Links
 - [Tweet](https://twitter.com/nuxt_js/status/1507378100614668302)
 - [Docs](https://v3.nuxtjs.org/docs/usage/cli/#add)
 - [Example Templates](https://github.com/nuxt/framework/blob/main/packages/nuxi/src/utils/templates.ts)

title: Improve image cache-control header when using minimumCacheTTL
body:
### Describe the feature you'd like to request

By default, next returns `cache-control: public, max-age=0, must-revalidate` for non-immutable images.
My expectation when using `minimumCacheTTL` is for the header to be `cache-control: public, max-age=${Math.max(minimumCacheTTL, originMaxAge)}, must-revalidate`.

This should help efficiency:
* reduce the amount of `304` responses / etag checks
* setups where there is a single cache / load balancer in front of multiple next instances would use less cpu (no need to generate an image multiple times if it was already generated by a different instance)

### Describe the solution you'd like

When `minimumCacheTTL` is present, the headers next sends get set to `cache-control: public, max-age=${Math.max(minimumCacheTTL, originMaxAge)}, must-revalidate`.

Alternatively, a flag in the settings which forces this behavior (eg. `forceExplicitCacheHeaders` or similar).

### Describe alternatives you've considered

Using `headers` to do this "manually" - however [this is advised against by the docs](https://nextjs.org/docs/api-reference/next/image#minimum-cache-ttl=).

title: Support SSG (one-time SSR) during runtime
body:
### Describe the feature you'd like to request

In some cases it would be quite useful to allow the rendering of a specific (non dynamic) page to happen on the server side during the runtime and that the render result will be cached for future requests. 

The "delayed" rendering of pages works if you have dynamic routes with [ISR](https://nextjs.org/docs/basic-features/data-fetching/incremental-static-regeneration) but doesn't work for specific pages.

Having some functionality that would allow the one-time rendering of specific pages on the server side would give developers the possibility to build applications that make use of configurations that are only available on the server side.

A typical use case could be CMS based application that gets build as a Docker image and should be stateless so that the runtime environment can provide the specific environment variables like secrets for the CMS. These secrets shouldn't be packaged in something like a Docker image but provided during runtime.

### Describe the solution you'd like

Allow `getStaticProps` to compile a specific (non dynamic) page on the server side and utilise the same caching mechanism like we have for ISR.

### Describe alternatives you've considered

_Nothing in mind right now._

title: Eslint: Allow importing next/server types outside of middleware functions
body:
### Describe the feature you'd like to request

I'd like to be better able to modularize my code in `_middleware.ts` functions.

### Describe the solution you'd like

I'd like to be able to `import type { NextRequest, NextResponse } from 'next/server'` outside of `_middleware.ts` functions.

### Describe alternatives you've considered

I've considered creating encapsulation type in a single file, with this rule ignored, so that I can avoid proliferating eslint ignores throughout my codebase

_types.ts_
```ts
// eslint-disable-next-line @next/next/no-server-import-in-page
import type { NextRequest, NextResponse } from 'next/server

export type MiddlewareHandler = (req: NextRequest, resp: NextResponse): NextResponse
```

__middleware.ts_
```ts
export async function middleware(req: NextRequest) {
    const response = NextResponse.next()
    return handleAuthentication(req, response)
}
```

title: server side global state that's request-private?
body:
where request = execution context for rendering of a page (whether at build time for SSG, or at runtime for SSR)

Basically, I want to ensure that a react-external server-side global state store instance<sup>1</sup> is _never_ shared across requests.

Looking through examples, I've come across variations of this pattern: https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/canary/examples/with-redux/src/app/store.ts#L24
https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/canary/examples/with-redux/src/pages/_app.tsx#L6,L10

but that seems flawed as the `app/store.ts` module factory executes once for the lifetime of the server process... so all requests will end up sharing the exported same store instance. Previous requests potentially leaking request/user sensitive data to subsequent ones is scary.

I tried doing something like `initStore` inside each page's `getStaticProps` to get a fresh store instance for each request/page... but then I got blocked when it came to accessing the same instance in `pages/_app`.

I tried threading it via `props` returned from each page's `getStaticProps` to `pageProps` passed to the react component in `pages/_app`... but that requires the value being serializable.

I also tried looking for request/page lifecycle methods that I could leverage and came up empty.

Is there some way to achieve this with next.js?

<sub>1: that's a lot of qualifiers :P ... I couldn't figure out how else to phrase this. suggestions welcome!</sub>

title: How to dynamically set "next/image" priority?
body:
Hey everybody, I'm Marcel and I've been working with Next.js for quite some time now.

Recently I came across the priority prop of next/image (https://nextjs.org/docs/api-reference/next/image#priority) while working on a personal project.

I do understand how it works and how it can be useful for pages where you know exactly what images are going to be above the fold.

However, imagine the scenario of CSS-TRICKS for instance, more specifically a /tag/tagName URL like "[css-tricks.com/tag/view-source/](https://css-tricks.com/tag/view-source/)" for example. While using a 1920x1080 monitor like mine, the user can see that 3 articles loaded above the fold and each of their "featured images" should have the priority prop set to true (please check screenshot) I know this scenario could vary if you're using a different size monitor, a phone, a tablet or whatever.

My question is, is there a way to identify which images are going to be above the fold to specify which ones should be prioritized like in this case? Well, if you know how to deal with this specific scenario, please let me know.

I'm asking this because my personal project has a very similar scenario with tens of divs and each one has its featured image and I'd like for the ones that are above the fold to have their images preloaded, no matter the client device.

author: haifn

title: Support `no-default-exports` workflows
body:
### Describe the feature you'd like to request

Our style guide enforces `import/no-default-export`. We find this helps with TypeScript IntelliSense (auto-completion) in IDEs like VSCode.

Right now, Next.js requires default exports in pages and API routes.

### Describe the solution you'd like

Where Next.js expects default exports today, we'd like the option of using named exports.

For example:
```tsx
// pages/my-page.tsx

export function Page ()  { /* .. */ }

export async function getServerSideProps () { /* .. */ }
```

### Describe alternatives you've considered

We could, as an alternative, disable this rule for anything in the pages directory (and in any other places it would be needed).

author: iuzn

title: [Feature Request] Allow tags to always be emitted to HTML stream during SSR, just like react-helmet-async's `prioritizeSeoTags`
body:
### Describe the feature you'd like to request

With Stream SSR, the content of `next/head` may not be emitted into `renderToPipeableStream` stream, since the component that uses `next/head` may not be processed by ReactDOM when `</head>` is written. This is bad for SEO and sharing on social media / IM (where the Open Graph metadata is needed to show the rich information).

With a prop (I propose `<Head priority={true} />`), Next.js should collect those tags before the `</head>` is being written.

### Describe the solution you'd like

The React 18 WG has provided a guide: https://github.com/reactwg/react-18/discussions/106

### Describe alternatives you've considered

Expose an API that works with custom `_app.js`, which allows Next.js to discover the content of the head that needs to be written to HTML as soon as possible, then the `</>` can be emitted as soon as possible.

title: Why is params optional in GetStaticPropsContext type?
body:
This is from [https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/.../packages/next/types/index.d.ts](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/937ab16b780dc2bc755fa9c032ffe498c79b5af6/packages/next/types/index.d.ts):

```ts
export type GetStaticPropsContext<
  Q extends ParsedUrlQuery = ParsedUrlQuery,
  D extends PreviewData = PreviewData
> = {
  params?: Q
  preview?: boolean
  previewData?: D
  locale?: string
  locales?: string[]
  defaultLocale?: string
}
```

Why is `params` optional? Correct me if I'm wrong, but it will always be provided to `getStaticProps()`. 

Right now we're forced to do a non-null assertion in order for it to be correctly typed, which is annoying, why not making it non-optional?

title: Cache inconsistencies occur in Vercel and Next.js
body:
[Question]
Is it possible that the main chunk has the same hash, but the hash of the static chunk it requests is different?
Since the hash is the same and cache-control is immutable, it is strange that the hash of the chunk to be GET is different. Is the hash of the chunk being requested not included in the main chunk?

Example...
The value of the hash part has no meaning and is dummy.

* In Chrome (Private Mode)
From the Network tab, Initiator

dummy.vercel.app/app/_next/static/chunks/main-abc123 (age: 100  cache-control: public, max-age:31536000, immutable)
-- dummy.vercel.app/app/_next/static/chunks/000-123xxx.js (age: 100)
-- dummy.vercel.app/app/_next/static/chunks/555-a12xxx.js (age: 100)
-- dummy.vercel.app/app/_next/static/chunks/999-z99xxx.js (age: 100)


* In Chrome (Default Mode)
From the Network tab, Initiator

dummy.vercel.app/app/_next/static/chunks/main-abc123（age: 100  cache-control: public, max-age:31536000, immutable）
-- dummy.vercel.app/app/_next/static/chunks/999-a12xxx.js (age: 10000)
-- dummy.vercel.app/app/_next/static/chunks/222-b33xxx.js (age: 10000)
-- dummy.vercel.app/app/_next/static/chunks/444-c56xxx.js (age: 10000)

(asset_prefix=app)

---

In Chrome / Safari, page transitions by Link do not work in normal browsers with caches of previously deployed apps, even though they work fine in private mode.
In private mode, prefetch makes the transition smooth, but in cached environments, the transition takes a long time. Perhaps getStaticProps is being executed from the beginning each time.
When I remove Chrome's cache, the hash of the chunk that the main chunk GETS is up-to-date, and the page transition by Link is smooth.

I was curious and checked the chunks being prefetched in the Developer Tool, and found that the hash of the main chunk from the request source was the same, but the hash of the chunk that the main chunk GETs was different.
This led me to believe that a cache inconsistency was occurring.
I did not change the Next.js or Vercel configuration, I just changed the source code of the app and deployed it. This has never happened before.

Is there any way to resolve this issue? I have tried several deployments with slightly different source code, but the same event occurs.

[Provide environment information]
Relevant packages:
next: 12.1.1-canary.3
react: 17.0.2
react-dom: 17.0.2

[What browser are you using? (if relevant)]
Version 99.0.4844.83 (Official Build) (arm64)

[How are you deploying your application? (if relevant)]
next build (Vercel Production Deployment)

[Expected Behavior]
The hash of the chunk retrieved by the main chunk is updated each time it is deployed, even on browsers with old caches. Cache inconsistencies should not occur.

[To Reproduce]
unknown

title: Allow developers to specify additional paths for output file tracing
body:
### Describe the feature you'd like to request

Because Next doesn't offer a way to run tasks just before server startup, developers need to resort to `NODE_OPTIONS="--require preload.js"` to  support certain tasks like open telemetry initialisation.

Unfortunately, this `preload.js` file and it dependencies will not be traced as part of outputFileTracing. We can write a standalone script that mimics `trace-next-server`, but it is largely duplicative.

### Describe the solution you'd like

`trace-next-server` makes a call to `nodeFileTrace` with an array comprising a single path to `nextServerPath`:

https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/ff474b01db0ee2ed176b046fc2d0c891bc4efa88/scripts/trace-next-server.js#L73

I would like to propose a new configuration parameter `outputFileTracingExtraPaths`, an array of paths to additional paths that will also be traced.

This array would be appended to the array sent to nodeFileTrace, ensuring that the files and their dependencies make it into next-server-js.nft.json

The paths would also be included in the standalone bundle.

### Describe alternatives you've considered

* Writing our own complementary version of `trace-next-server` (our current approach): lots of duplication, and keeping it up to date is brittle.
* `unstable_includePaths: ['preload.js']` in the export of `pages/index.tsx`: this includes the preload file itself in the standalone output, but none of its `require'd` dependencies. it's also a bit strange.

title: Prettier to ESLint changes
body:
Hello,

I've started learning React recently and I had a project that was using `12.1.0`  that shipped with a `prettier.config.js`  formatted to
```js
module.exports = {
    singleQuote: true,
    semi: false,
}
```
Now it seems it uses `ESLint` to do the formatting but the defaults seem different. This means that the header that ships with `npx create-next-app@latest` is not correctly formatted out of the box to `ESLint`. 

```js
import Head from 'next/head'
import Image from 'next/image'
import styles from '../styles/Home.module.css'
```

Then when I press save it all changes to:
```js
import Head from "next/head";
import Image from "next/image";
import styles from "../styles/Home.module.css";
```

There are a few other changes such as:
`Get started by editing{' '}` changes to `Get started by editing{" "}` and `Powered by{' '}` changes to `Powered by{" "}`.

I know it sounds very trivial but it was something that had me curious about the reason behind the change? Is it to be more inline with best-practice? Also why doesn't the default files shipped formatted correctly?

title: babelrc
body:
### Verify canary release

- [X] I verified that the issue exists in Next.js canary release

### Provide environment information

```
$ npx next info

    Operating System:
      Platform: darwin
      Arch: arm64
      Version: Darwin Kernel Version 21.4.0: Mon Feb 21 20:36:53 PST 2022; root:xnu-8020.101.4~2/RELEASE_ARM64_T8101

      react: 17.0.2
      react-dom: 17.0.2
```

### What browser are you using? (if relevant)

_No response_

### How are you deploying your application? (if relevant)

_No response_

### Describe the Bug

[Babel allows](https://babeljs.io/docs/en/configuration#packagejson) configuration via either the `.babelrc` file or the `babel` field inside `package.json`.  `nextjs` detects the former but not the latter.  In the following (using the canary build), we see this line `info  - Disabled SWC as replacement for Babel because of custom Babel configuration ".babelrc"` if `.babelrc` is present but not if it is in the babel field is in `package.json`

```bash
$ > yarn build  
yarn run v1.22.17
$ next build
info  - Loaded env from /Volumes/Workspace/aerialops/.env.local
info  - Checking validity of types  
info  - Disabled SWC as replacement for Babel because of custom Babel configuration ".babelrc" https://nextjs.org/docs/messages/swc-disabled
info  - Using external babel configuration from /Volumes/Workspace/aerialops/.babelrc
info  - Creating an optimized production build  
[ ... ]
✨  Done in 8.56s.
$ > rm .babelrc 
remove .babelrc? y
$ > yarn build # babel in package.json
yarn run v1.22.17
$ next build
info  - Loaded env from /Volumes/Workspace/aerialops/.env.local
info  - Checking validity of types  
info  - Creating an optimized production build  
info  - Compiled successfully
info  - Collecting page data  
info  - Generating static pages (4/4)
info  - Finalizing page optimization  
[ ... ]
```

### Expected Behavior

[Babel allows](https://babeljs.io/docs/en/configuration#packagejson) configuration via either the `.babelrc` file or the `babel` field inside `package.json`.  Expect `nextjs` to pick this up in both.

### To Reproduce

Move contents of `.babelrc` into `package.json`'s `babel` field and run build.

title: node_modules unnecessarily excluded in tsconfig
body:
### Verify canary release

- [X] I verified that the issue exists in Next.js canary release

### Provide environment information

```
    Operating System:
      Platform: linux
      Arch: x64
      Version: #55-Ubuntu SMP Wed Jan 12 17:36:34 UTC 2022
    Binaries:
      Node: 17.8.0
      npm: 8.5.5
      Yarn: 1.22.18
      pnpm: N/A
    Relevant packages:
      next: 12.1.5-canary.0
      react: 18.0.0
      react-dom: 18.0.0
```

### What browser are you using? (if relevant)

Not relevant.

### How are you deploying your application? (if relevant)

Not relevant.

### Describe the Bug

Next unnecessarily writes `"exclude": ["node_modules"]` when creating a new TypeScript project (i.e., with `npx create-next-app@latest --ts`) or to the project's existing `tsconfig.json`. 

```
We detected TypeScript in your project and reconfigured your tsconfig.json file for you. Strict-mode is set to false by default.

The following suggested values were added to your tsconfig.json. These values can be changed to fit your project's needs:

        - exclude was set to ['node_modules']
```

### Expected Behavior

Next should not do this because:
1. TypeScript already ignores `node_modules` by default (see https://www.typescriptlang.org/tsconfig#exclude).
2. This configuration is _incorrect_ if `node_modules` is not located in the current directory (as is the case when using npm workspaces, for example).

### To Reproduce

1. Create a new Next prject with `npx create-next-app@latest --ts`

Or

1. Run `next dev` in an existing Next TypeScript project where `exclude` is not defined in the project's `tsconfig.json`.

title: Can't consume webpack library in application
body:
### Verify canary release

- [X] I verified that the issue exists in Next.js canary release

### Provide environment information

next.js 12.1.4
webpack 5.70.0

### What browser are you using? (if relevant)

in any browser

### How are you deploying your application? (if relevant)

-

### Describe the Bug

Hello, i'm trying to build lib by webpack for consume in different applications

Min problem - i don't understand where is something wrong, only symptoms. Researching problem in react, webpack and now i hope next.js community give me some explanation or advices.

In a nutshell:
Demo project structure:
```
packages
  sdk
    store/call <-- contains useCall and withCall. Call @company/sdk-shared/react/devices.useCall
    store/devices <-- contain useDevices and withDevices
    webpack.config.js
       entry -> store/call
       entry -> store/devices
  ui
    dynamic-component
      useDevices() < -- return valid store
      useCall() <-- return valid store
        useDevices() <-- return undefined (only nested useDevices doesn't work)
```

I really need two different endpoint for avoid loading call imports when use devices. It's improtant thing

First try:
In [this react issue](https://github.com/facebook/react/issues/24230#issuecomment-1084339972) i understand problem and try to split my code into two packages, but problem not disappeared, sad

Second try:
I'm tried to find solution in webpack documentation and community. Best option that i find - webpack.entry.dependOn. But it does't work for library i guess. My research and demo about this accumulated in [webpack issue - an't create webpack module with dependOn for import ](https://github.com/webpack/webpack/issues/15616)

I know, next.js widely use webpack and will be appreciate for any help with webpack entry `dependOn` or solutions for compose react hooks in npm packages built by webpack (or other solution)

PS: i chose webpack, because other build tools can't support typescript project references. Webpack works with this very well, but with webpack i can't build working package :(

Related issues with discussions:
1. https://github.com/facebook/react/issues/24230
2. https://github.com/webpack/webpack/issues/15605
3. https://github.com/webpack/webpack/issues/15616



### Expected Behavior

Nested hook useDevices works fine in hook useCall.

### To Reproduce

[My reproducible example](https://github.com/lavcraft/react-hooks-composition-in-webpack-entry-problem-example)

1. `git clone git@github.com:lavcraft/react-hooks-composition-in-webpack-entry-problem-example.git`
2. `cd react-hooks-composition-in-webpack-entry-problem-example`
4. `npm ci`
5. `npm run all:start` (if you have tmux. It start session with all required processes). Instead - run manually `npm run sdk:dev` and then `npm run ui:dev`

After that, `open http://localhost:3000` and see error like this in console
```
Uncaught Error: Use useDevices must be used only within withDevices
```

In branch `checkout depends-on-experiments` no dependOn. Just separated package sdk-shared, which contain `useDevices` hook as advised before in react issue. And this doesn't work too or i do something wrong :(

title: [Tree Shake] Next.js emit unused code into the `main` bundle
body: ### Verify canary release - [X] I verified that the issue exists in Next.js canary release ### Provide environment information Operating System: Platform: darwin Arch: x64 Version: Darwin Kernel Version 21.4.0: Mon Feb 21 20:34:37 PST 2022; root:xnu-8020.101.4~2/RELEASE_X86_64 Binaries: Node: 16.14.0 npm: 8.5.1 Yarn: 1.22.18 pnpm: 6.32.4 Relevant packages: next: 12.1.5-canary.1 react: 18.0.0 react-dom: 18.0.0 ### What browser are you using? (if relevant) _No response_ ### How are you deploying your application? (if relevant) next build / export ### Describe the Bug I notice the `main` bundle emitted by `next build` is a bit heavy, so I use `@next/bundle-analyzer` to analyze my bundle: And there are many features I don't use at all that are included in the bundle: **next/dist/shared/amp.js** I don't use the AMP feature at all. So I take a look at the source code. `shared/lib/amp.ts` has 2 named export, `useAmp` and `isInAmpMode`: https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/0eb9f7e76d0d7ec6aa7d85d3eaf57b151f381fb3/packages/next/shared/lib/amp.ts#L4-L15 `isInAmpMode` is then imported by `shared/lib/head.tsx`: https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/fbfe430cf0d21513eadb7be7e9c05a6ac6d5048a/packages/next/shared/lib/head.tsx#L5 I do use `next/head` component, so it is very reasonable that `isInAmpMode` is included in my bundle. However, `useAmp` is also included in the final bundle: **next/dist/client/script.js** I don't use `next/script` component at all. And the `scriptLoader` property of the `__NEXT_DATA__` is also an empty array: So I take a look at the source code again. `client/script.tsx` has a default export and a named export `initScriptLoader`: https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/fbfe430cf0d21513eadb7be7e9c05a6ac6d5048a/packages/next/client/script.tsx#L132-L134 ...which is then imported by `client/index.tsx`: https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/0eb9f7e76d0d7ec6aa7d85d3eaf57b151f381fb3/packages/next/client/index.tsx#L254-L257 However, the entire `next/dist/client/script.js` is still included in the final `main` bundle: > As you can see there are keywords like `text/partydown`, `afterInteractive` and `lazyOnLoad`. **next/dist/client/streaming/vitals.js** I do use `reportWebVitals` named export on my custom `_app`, but I don't use `unstable_useWebVitalsReport`. So once again, I take a look at the source code of `next.js`. There are tons of named export at `client/streaming/vitals.ts`: https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/0eb9f7e76d0d7ec6aa7d85d3eaf57b151f381fb3/packages/next/client/streaming/vitals.ts#L5 https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/0eb9f7e76d0d7ec6aa7d85d3eaf57b151f381fb3/packages/next/client/streaming/vitals.ts#L10-L12 https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/0eb9f7e76d0d7ec6aa7d85d3eaf57b151f381fb3/packages/next/client/streaming/vitals.ts#L19-L22 https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/0eb9f7e76d0d7ec6aa7d85d3eaf57b151f381fb3/packages/next/client/streaming/vitals.ts#L24 `flushBufferedVitalsMetrics` and `trackWebVitalMetric` are imported by `client/index.tsx`: https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/0eb9f7e76d0d7ec6aa7d85d3eaf57b151f381fb3/packages/next/client/index.tsx#L36-L39 ... which is used by `` component: https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/0eb9f7e76d0d7ec6aa7d85d3eaf57b151f381fb3/packages/next/client/index.tsx#L1032-L1036 ... and `hydrate` function: https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/0eb9f7e76d0d7ec6aa7d85d3eaf57b151f381fb3/packages/next/client/index.tsx#L323 However, `useWebVitalsReport` is also included in my final bundle: **next/dist/client/streaming/refresh.js** I am pretty sure I don't use `useRefreshRoot` at all. And Next.js itself doesn't uses `useRefreshRoot` either. However, it is still included in the final bundle: ### Expected Behavior All those unused codes I mentioned above should not be included in the final `main` bundle. ### To Reproduce I introduce `@next/bundle-analyzer` into another bare bone Next.js reproduction (the one I used in https://github.com/vercel/next.js/issues/35286), and all those unused codes are included in the `main` bundle:

title: Relay language config
body:
### Describe the feature you'd like to request

The docs show how it is possible to use Next (@latest) with Relay (v13) and it works super!
https://nextjs.org/docs/advanced-features/compiler#relay

Nevertheless, Relay v14 (@master) has the possibility to emit types for different languages, before it was only possible `flow` and `typescript` but now has been added `javascript` language (no types emitted of course).


```
wait  - compiling...
error - ./node_modules/next/dist/client/dev/amp-dev.js
Error: failed to deserialize as next_swc::TransformOptions
JSON: {"jsc":{"parser":{"syntax":"ecmascript","dynamicImport":true,"decorators":false,"jsx":true,"importAssertions":true},"experimental":{"keepImportAssertions":true},"transform":{"legacyDecorator":false,"decoratorMetadata":false,"react":{"importSource":"react","runtime":"automatic","pragm

// ...

Caused by:
    unknown variant `javascript`, expected `typescript` or `flow` at line 1 column 981
```

### Describe the solution you'd like

Could be possible add to the config parser for `language` also `javascript`?
Mainly in the **canary** version would be fine.


### Describe alternatives you've considered

Waiting for next Relay release would take too long while we try to test any new feature in the whole stack.
May be @alunyov could give us some ETA for Relay, or even better confirm that `javascript` configuration option is stable and ongoing to be part of next release.

author: VianaArthur

title: Add es-check as CI step in Next pipeline
body:
### Describe the feature you'd like to request

Next currently officially supports ES5 builds for client code: https://github.com/vercel/next.js/discussions/33227

There have been a few recent bugs related to shipping non-ES5 code in releases:
- https://github.com/vercel/next.js/issues/35912
- https://github.com/vercel/next.js/issues/34629
- https://github.com/vercel/next.js/issues/33314

As a consumer of Next since v5, it seems like the frequency of these regressions has been increasing recently, which is understandable as modern JS moves further and further from ES5. Next should have a CI step that prevents the introduction of these regressions as long as ES5 is the official target. This would prevent consumers from getting blocked on upgrades and having to report these bugs and then waiting for the next release (which can be understandably slow).

### Describe the solution you'd like

[es-check](https://github.com/yowainwright/es-check) is a lightweight _fast_ utility that is capable of preventing these types of regressions. Next should introduce its usage as a CI step. The easiest path forward would be running it against the built static output in pre-existing steps (like the e2e or production test suites maybe?). Maybe use the existing open https://github.com/vercel/next.js/issues/35912 ticket as a way to test efficacy, since its codepath (`next/dynamic`)  isn't necessarily invoked in many setups.

### Describe alternatives you've considered

es-check is the defacto tool in this situation, as evidenced by all of the bug reports mentioning it. It's possible that this could be caught in some sort of browser grid testing, but that would be much slower and might not address all browsers Next consumers are targeting while relying on ES5.

author: Glutch

title: Use getInitialProps with getServersideProps
body:
### Describe the feature you'd like to request

The ability to use both getInitialProps and getServersideProps for Relay preloading. Some data I want to come instantly loaded from the server. Additionally I may want to do some serverside gating / logging. getServersideProps works great for this. However, for Relay's usePreloadedQuery I want to call loadQuery on the client in getInitialProps. This allows query fetching to start right away on the client, but also doesn't block the initial page load on it fully loading (As would be the case with server-side loading the query, or SSR).

### Describe the solution you'd like

getInitialProps to not be blocked when getServersideProps is in use. Additionally, if getInitialProps JS was somehow downloaded independent of page UI, since it is independent logic that can start running as soon as a page transition starts (perhaps I can use dynamic import for this, but I don't want to *delay* the loading of UI code, only *split* it out)

One issue with getInitialProps is for first page load its run on the server and serialized. I could perhaps serialize the results of the query in this case only, but it would be nice if I could just run this on the client as well.

### Describe alternatives you've considered

1. Move getInitialProps logic into getServersideProps

This doesn't work for starting a fetch clientside. The server cannot kick off a network request on the client.

2. SSR the query (and pass the query results to the client)

This requires blocking the whole page load on the result of the query

3. Load the query in a useEffect at the top of my page component

This is an anti-pattern discouraged by relay, as it means the network request doesn't get sent to the server until the full page is downloaded and finishes an entire first render.

body:
I'm working on a heavy nextJs project (over 1mb gzipped) and on the latest version of nextJs (and node).
Memory usage (and build time) during dev mode has been climbing exponentially. First I had to allocate >4GB to node, which then became 8GB, and now I cannot even launch without a 16GB heap. If I didn't find the following fix I would have had no choice but to abandon using the nextJs tooling as I don't have more memory (or time) to throw at this problem.

Thankfully, the following fix in next.config.js has returned dev mode to using ~1gb mem and compiling pages very quickly:

```
  experimental: {
    esmExternals: false,
  },
```

I hope that this information might be of use to someone else. I'm not going to bother raising an issue about this as there are already numerous issues about memory usage that are unresolved.

Note:
Running 'next build' 'next start' has no memory or performance issues at all, it's simply dev mode that has this issue.

title: Hide Warning about Automatic Static Optimization
body:
### Describe the feature you'd like to request

A setting to hide following warning:

```
Warning: You have opted-out of Automatic Static Optimization due to `getInitialProps` in `pages/_app`. This does not opt-out pages with `getStaticProps`
Read more: https://nextjs.org/docs/messages/opt-out-auto-static-optimization
```

https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/4a7ab34baf932c102fbf281660f09f7eb98c4b74/packages/next/build/index.ts#L1141

I know i use it and i use it because i have to as i need to get settings from api on each call. So i just want to disable this warning. For me, too many warnings during build just hides the relevant warnings.

### Describe the solution you'd like

Maybe a setting in `next.config.js` for all warnings to disable them. Maybe it can also be used to throw an error instead of warning if wanted (if someone wants to):

```
const nextConfig = {
  warnings: {
    staticOptimization: 'off' | 'warn' | 'error',
    ...
  }
}
```

### Describe alternatives you've considered

it may also be changed using a setting in the `.env` file

title: Add support for configurable `jsxImportSource` when using Emotion SWC
body:
### Describe the feature you'd like to request

Currently, when opting into the SWC Emotion plugin, there is an assumption made that the `jsxImportSource` is `@emotion/react`. This prevents projects that use Emotion 10 from being able to use SWC, because the package which provides the JSX runtime is called `@emotion/core`.

### Describe the solution you'd like

I have a couple suggestions for how to resolve this.

1. Allow the `jsxImportSource` from `jsConfig.compilerOptions` to be used, if present
2. Allow a `jsxImportSource` to be specified as part of the `emotion` SWC options

Of these two suggestions, I think the first one is probably the better way to go, since its a little surprising that the `jsxImportSource` from `jsConfig.compilerOptions` is ignored if the `nextConfig.experimental.emotion` option is set

### Describe alternatives you've considered

I'm not aware of another way to set the `jsxImportSource` to support `@emotion/core`while using the SWC Emotion plugin
